cases.title: Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction from ABO-Incompatible RBC Transfusion Due to Clerical Error
domains: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
cases.clinical_setup: A 54-year-old man with an upper GI bleed is started on his first unit of crossmatched RBCs on a general medicine floor. The unit was retrieved by a nurse covering for a colleague during a busy shift change; the bedside identification check was performed quickly. About 10 minutes into the transfusion, the patient becomes acutely uncomfortable, reporting chills, severe flank and low back pain, and a sense of "impending doom." He is visibly diaphoretic and anxious. The nurse notes his blood pressure has dropped and his Foley bag now contains dark, tea-colored urine that was clear an hour earlier.

findings.label: Temperature
findings.value: 38.6°C, risen from a pre-transfusion baseline of 37.0°C, with associated rigors

findings.label: Reported symptoms
findings.value: Flank/back pain, chills, sense of impending doom

findings.label: Urine appearance (Foley)
findings.value: Dark red/tea-colored, new since transfusion start

findings.label: Post-reaction plasma inspection
findings.value: Visibly pink/red discoloration consistent with hemoglobinemia

findings.label: Repeat ABO/Rh grouping (patient vs. implicated unit)
findings.value: Patient types as group O; the transfused unit retypes as group A — ABO discrepancy identified, confirming the wrong unit was administered

findings.label: Urinalysis
findings.value: Hemoglobinuria (dipstick blood-positive with few/no intact RBCs on microscopy)

decision_points.prompt: Ten minutes into the transfusion the patient develops fever, chills, flank pain, and hypotension. What is the single most important immediate bedside action?
options: Slow the infusion rate and reassess in 15 minutes
Give acetaminophen and continue the transfusion
Stop the transfusion immediately, keep the IV open with 0.9% saline, perform a clerical recheck, and notify the physician/blood bank
Switch to an antihistamine and observe for resolution of hives
decision_points.answer: Stop the transfusion immediately, maintain IV access with 0.9% saline, perform an immediate clerical recheck at the bedside, and notify the physician and blood bank.
decision_points.rationale: This constellation — fever, chills, flank/back pain, hypotension, and a feeling of impending doom — is the classic presentation of an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction. The mandated initial bedside action for any suspected transfusion reaction of this severity is to stop the transfusion, keep venous access open with normal saline, perform a clerical recheck, and notify the physician and blood bank immediately (SRC-045).

decision_points.prompt: After stopping the transfusion, what diagnostic workup should be sent to confirm or refute a hemolytic transfusion reaction, and what is the leading root cause to investigate?
decision_points.answer: Visually inspect the post-reaction plasma for hemoglobinemia, send a post-transfusion DAT, and repeat ABO/Rh grouping on both the patient's sample and the implicated unit as part of a full clerical recheck. Clerical/identification error ("wrong blood in tube"/patient misidentification) is the leading, often entirely preventable, cause of acute intravascular hemolytic reactions and must be actively investigated.
decision_points.rationale: The standard lab workup for a suspected hemolytic reaction is visual inspection of plasma for hemoglobinemia, a DAT on the post-transfusion sample, and repeat ABO grouping (SRC-045). Clerical errors remain the primary cause of acute intravascular hemolytic reactions, which is why barcode/electronic ID systems exist to reduce wrong-blood-in-tube events (SRC-032; SRC-045).

decision_points.prompt: Results return: pink plasma, positive DAT, decreased haptoglobin, elevated LDH and indirect bilirubin, spherocytes, and hemoglobinuria, with an ABO discrepancy confirming a group A unit was given to a group O patient. Which category and mechanism of hemolysis does this represent, and how does it differ from a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction (DHTR)?
decision_points.answer: This is an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR): intravascular, complement-mediated hemolysis, classically triggered by IgM anti-A/anti-B in ABO-incompatible transfusion, occurring during or within 24 hours of transfusion. DHTR, by contrast, is typically extravascular and IgG-mediated (an anamnestic alloantibody response), presenting with a positive DAT and a newly identified alloantibody or positive eluate 24 hours to 28 days after transfusion.
decision_points.rationale: AHTR is usually intravascular (IgM/complement) and immediate, evidenced here by hemoglobinuria, decreased haptoglobin, elevated LDH/bilirubin, and spherocytes; DHTR is usually extravascular (IgG) and occurs days later with a positive DAT and newly identified alloantibody or eluate (SRC-045). This alloimmune, ABO-specific pattern also differs from an autoimmune process: in warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia the eluate is characteristically panreactive against essentially all cells tested, whereas an alloantibody-mediated reaction like this one reflects a specific ABO incompatibility rather than a panreactive autoantibody (SRC-061).

decision_points.prompt: The patient remains hypotensive with declining urine output. What are the priority management steps, and can this transfusion episode be resumed once he stabilizes?
options: Resume the same unit slowly once blood pressure improves
Restrict fluids to avoid worsening hemolysis
Give vigorous fluid resuscitation to maintain urine output >1 mL/kg/hr, treat shock/DIC as needed, and permanently discard the implicated unit — do not resume
Only administer antihistamines and monitor
decision_points.answer: Give vigorous IV fluid resuscitation targeting urine output >1 mL/kg/hr, treat shock and DIC if they develop, and do not resume the implicated unit under any circumstances. Report the event through institutional hemovigilance as a Definitive-imputability, severe (or life-threatening) reaction; a fatal outcome would require FDA notification.
decision_points.rationale: AHTR management centers on vigorous fluid resuscitation to maintain urine output above 1 mL/kg/hr and treatment of shock and DIC (SRC-045). Resuming the unit is a classic board trap: transfusion may only be restarted after successful treatment of an isolated, simple urticarial reaction with no other symptoms — it is never appropriate for fever, chills, pain, or any hemolytic reaction (SRC-045). Under NHSN hemovigilance criteria this event would be graded Definitive imputability (fulfills case-definition criteria) and Severe/Life-threatening based on clinical course, and any transfusion-related fatality must be reported to the FDA (CBER) as soon as possible with a written report to follow within 7 days (SRC-032).

cases.answer_key: Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction (AHTR) due to ABO incompatibility from a clerical ("wrong blood in tube"/patient-misidentification) error. Correct pathway: (1) STOP the transfusion immediately, keep the IV open with 0.9% saline, perform an immediate bedside clerical recheck, and notify the physician and blood bank; (2) send a post-reaction sample for visual inspection of plasma (hemoglobinemia), a post-transfusion DAT, and a repeat ABO/Rh type-and-recheck on both patient and implicated unit; (3) recognize the resulting pattern — positive DAT, pink/red plasma, decreased haptoglobin, elevated LDH and indirect bilirubin, spherocytes, and hemoglobinuria — as intravascular, complement-mediated hemolysis typical of AHTR (as opposed to the extravascular, IgG-mediated, delayed pattern of DHTR); (4) treat with vigorous fluid resuscitation targeting urine output >1 mL/kg/hr, manage shock/DIC as needed, permanently discontinue (do NOT resume) the implicated unit, and report the event through institutional hemovigilance with a Definitive imputability/severe-to-life-threatening grading, escalating to FDA notification if the outcome were fatal.
cases.teaching_explanation: Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR) is accelerated RBC destruction occurring during or within 24 hours of transfusion, and clerical/patient-identification error is its leading, largely preventable cause — which is precisely why barcode and electronic identification systems exist. The classic presentation (fever, chills, flank/back pain, hypotension, and a sense of impending doom) reflects complement activation and intravascular hemolysis, most often driven by IgM anti-A/anti-B in ABO-incompatible transfusion. The confirmatory laboratory pattern is hemoglobinemia (pink/red plasma) and hemoglobinuria, decreased haptoglobin, elevated LDH and indirect bilirubin, spherocytes on smear, and a positive post-transfusion DAT with a repeat ABO discrepancy pinpointing the wrong-unit error. This intravascular/IgM/complement/immediate picture is the key contrast point against delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction (DHTR), which is extravascular, IgG-mediated (an anamnestic response to a previously formed alloantibody), and presents 24 hours to 28 days later with a positive DAT and a newly identified alloantibody or eluate. Management is supportive and urgent: stop the transfusion, keep the line open with saline, perform an immediate clerical recheck, notify the blood bank/physician, and then aggressively resuscitate with fluids to protect renal perfusion (urine output goal >1 mL/kg/hr) while monitoring for and treating shock or DIC. Unlike an isolated urticarial reaction — the only reaction type in which the same unit may be resumed after treatment — a hemolytic reaction always mandates permanent discontinuation of that unit. Once identified, the event is graded through hemovigilance frameworks (NHSN imputability and severity criteria) and, if fatal, must be reported to the FDA within defined timelines. Learners should also keep two related concepts in mind: hemolysis with a negative DAT points away from immune hemolysis toward nonimmune mechanical or thermal causes (small-bore needles, pumps, blood warmers, or inappropriate IV fluids like D5W), and in sickle cell disease patients a severe post-transfusion hemolytic event raises concern for hyperhemolysis syndrome, in which both donor and autologous RBCs are destroyed and further transfusion must be avoided.
pitfalls: Assuming isolated fever/chills during a transfusion is simply a febrile nonhemolytic transfusion reaction (FNHTR) without first excluding hemolysis via DAT, haptoglobin, LDH, and plasma inspection — AHTR must always be actively ruled out when fever/chills occur during transfusion.

Believing the transfusion can be restarted once the patient stabilizes: resuming the unit is only acceptable after successful treatment of an isolated, simple urticarial reaction with no other symptoms — never for fever, chills, pain, or any hemolytic process.

Forgetting that hemolysis with a negative DAT is not necessarily AHTR — nonimmune causes such as mechanical damage (small needles, pumps), improper temperature control (blood warmers), or infusion with an unapproved IV solution (e.g., D5W) can also produce hemolysis without an immune mechanism.

In a sickle cell disease patient with a severe post-transfusion hemolytic event, reflexively transfusing more RBCs to treat resulting anemia without considering hyperhemolysis syndrome, in which both donor and autologous RBCs are destroyed and further transfusion must be withheld.

Establishes that clerical errors are the primary, often preventable cause of acute intravascular hemolytic reactions, and provides the hemovigilance framework used in stage 4 (NHSN imputability designations such as Definitive, severity grading such as Severe/Life-threatening, and the requirement to report transfusion-related deaths to the FDA/CBER with a written report within 7 days).

Supports the teaching contrast in stage 3 between a specific alloantibody-mediated reaction (this case) and the panreactive eluate pattern characteristic of warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia, reinforcing that a positive DAT with a specific ABO discrepancy reflects alloimmune rather than autoimmune hemolysis.
Confirms that Hemolytic reactions are a listed subdomain within the Hazards of Transfusion exam domain, situating this case within the ABPath blueprint context.

cases.title: Fever, Flank Pain, and "Impending Doom" During Transfusion: Recognizing Acute Hemolytic Transfusion Reaction
domains: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
cases.clinical_setup: A 58-year-old man is being transfused one unit of RBCs for symptomatic anemia. Approximately 10 minutes into the infusion he develops rigors, low-back/flank pain, and tells the nurse he feels a sense of "impending doom." His temperature rises to 39.4°C, heart rate climbs to 128 bpm, and his urine turns red-brown over the next hour. The transfusion is stopped and a clerical/laboratory workup is initiated.

findings.label: Temperature
findings.value: 39.4°C, onset ~10 minutes into transfusion of the RBC unit

findings.label: Patient-reported symptoms
findings.value: Rigors/chills, low-back and flank pain, and a self-described sense of "impending doom"

findings.label: Urine appearance
findings.value: Red-brown urine, dipstick heme-positive without intact RBCs on microscopy — hemoglobinuria

findings.label: Renal function
findings.value: Rising creatinine with decreasing urine output over subsequent hours (evolving renal failure)

findings.label: Repeat ABO/Rh typing (clerical recheck)
findings.value: Archived pretransfusion patient sample types as Group O; the issued/transfused unit types as Group A — mismatch identified

findings.label: Direct Antiglobulin Test (DAT), post-reaction EDTA sample
findings.value: Positive with polyspecific AHG reagent, indicating in-vivo RBC coating with IgG and/or complement (C3d)

findings.label: Post-transfusion hemoglobin increment
findings.value: +0.2 g/dL after 1 RBC unit, versus the ~1 g/dL rise expected per unit in an average adult

decision_points.prompt: Ten minutes into the transfusion the patient develops fever, rigors, flank pain, a sense of "impending doom," and red-brown urine. What is the single most important immediate action?
options: Slow the infusion rate and reassess in 15 minutes
Stop the transfusion immediately, keep the IV line open with saline, and begin a clerical/patient-identity check while notifying the physician and blood bank
Give acetaminophen and continue the unit since fever alone is common
Draw a blood culture and continue infusing the remainder of the unit while awaiting results
decision_points.answer: Stop the transfusion immediately, maintain IV access with saline, and initiate a clerical/identity check while notifying the physician and blood bank.
decision_points.rationale: This constellation — fever, chills, hemoglobinuria, and a feeling of "impending doom," with risk of renal failure — is the classic presentation of acute intravascular hemolysis from ABO incompatibility (SRC-024). Because clerical errors (patient/sample/unit misidentification) remain the primary, largely preventable cause of acute intravascular hemolytic reactions (SRC-032), stopping the transfusion and verifying identity is both the safety-critical first step and the most efficient diagnostic step.

decision_points.prompt: The bedside wristband matches, but a mismatch was found between the patient's archived pretransfusion type and the issued unit. What should be sent to the blood bank to confirm the reaction and its cause?
options: Discard the unit and tubing per routine waste policy; no further testing needed once a clerical mismatch is found
Return the implicated unit and tubing along with a fresh EDTA post-transfusion patient sample for repeat ABO/Rh forward-and-reverse typing, a DAT, and an antibody screen/identification panel
Send only a clotted (serum) post-transfusion sample for the DAT
Repeat only the antibody screen; retyping is unnecessary once a clerical error is suspected
decision_points.answer: Return the unit/tubing plus a fresh EDTA post-transfusion sample for repeat ABO/Rh typing, DAT, and antibody screen/identification.
decision_points.rationale: Forward and reverse ABO/Rh typing is mandatory for confirming identity/compatibility problems (SRC-024), and the antibody screen/panel (using reagent O-cells) identifies any unexpected clinically significant antibody (SRC-024). The DAT detects in-vivo coating of RBCs with IgG or complement (SRC-032); EDTA is the preferred specimen for DAT because clotted specimens can show in-vitro complement attachment and yield a false positive (SRC-032). Any negative AHG/DAT result is only interpretable if IgG-coated check cells were added and reacted, confirming the reagent and technique were valid (SRC-032).

decision_points.prompt: The patient's post-transfusion hemoglobin rose only 0.2 g/dL (well below the ~1 g/dL expected per unit) and he required escalated inpatient care overnight but survived. How should this event be classified and handled from a hemovigilance standpoint?
options: No formal classification is needed since the patient ultimately survived

Grade the event using NHSN severity criteria (severe or life-threatening, given the escalation of care), assign an imputability designation, and document/report it through the hemovigilance system; note that FDA/CBER notification with a written report within 7 days is required only if the reaction results in death
Automatically classify as "Non-severe" because fever was the presenting symptom
Reporting obligations apply only when the causative antibody cannot be identified
decision_points.answer: Classify by NHSN severity (severe/life-threatening) and imputability criteria, document/report through hemovigilance, and apply FDA/CBER death-reporting timelines only if the reaction proves fatal.
decision_points.rationale: NHSN severity grading defines Severe as requiring/prolonging inpatient hospitalization and Life-threatening as immediate risk of death (SRC-032); imputability is graded Definitive/Probable/Possible/Doubtful based on how well the case meets criteria (SRC-032). Hemolytic reactions are among the transfusion reactions most frequently reported to the FDA as fatal (SRC-032), and suspected transfusion-related deaths specifically require prompt oral/electronic FDA notification followed by a written report within 7 days (SRC-032). The blunted hemoglobin increment (well under the expected ~1 g/dL per unit, per SRC-024/SRC-032) is itself objective evidence that the transfused cells were being destroyed, supporting the severity assessment.

cases.answer_key: The vignette is a clerical-error ABO-incompatible acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR). Correct pathway: (1) Recognize the classic acute intravascular hemolysis symptom complex — fever, chills, flank/low-back pain, "impending doom," and hemoglobinuria — occurring during transfusion, and immediately STOP the transfusion, keep the IV line open with saline, and begin a bedside clerical/identity check, since clerical error (misidentification) is the leading preventable cause of this reaction. (2) Confirm and characterize the reaction by returning the unit/tubing plus a fresh EDTA post-transfusion patient sample to the blood bank for repeat ABO/Rh forward-and-reverse typing (pre- vs post-transfusion), a DAT to detect in-vivo IgG/complement coating, and an antibody screen/panel — while remembering that a negative AHG result is only valid if IgG-coated check cells reacted. (3) Grade the event using NHSN severity (non-severe/severe/life-threatening/death) and imputability (definitive/probable/possible/doubtful) criteria, document it through the hemovigilance/nonconformance system, and, if the reaction had been fatal, notify FDA/CBER as soon as possible with a written follow-up within 7 days — recognizing that hemolytic reactions remain among the most frequently reported fatal transfusion complications.
cases.teaching_explanation: Hemolytic transfusion reactions are one of the leading causes of transfusion-related fatality reported to the FDA, alongside TRALI and TACO (SRC-032). The classic acute, intravascular presentation — most often from ABO incompatibility — produces fever, chills, hemoglobinuria, renal failure, and a feeling of "impending doom" (SRC-024), and the single most common preventable root cause is a clerical error in patient, sample, or unit identification rather than an exotic antibody (SRC-032). Recognizing this symptom cluster during transfusion should trigger an immediate stop, maintenance of IV access, and a bedside identity check before any further laboratory workup. Confirmatory testing leans on core immunohematology tools: repeat forward/reverse ABO-Rh typing to catch mislabeling (SRC-024), a DAT to demonstrate in-vivo IgG/complement coating of red cells (SRC-032), and an antibody screen/panel to identify a causative alloantibody (SRC-024). Two technical pitfalls matter here: DAT specimens should be EDTA rather than clotted serum, since clotted samples can develop in-vitro complement attachment and falsely suggest hemolysis (SRC-032); and any negative AHG-based test (DAT or antibody screen) is only valid once IgG-coated check cells have been shown to react (SRC-032). Once a reaction is confirmed, it is formally graded through the NHSN hemovigilance framework — severity (non-severe/severe/life-threatening/death) and imputability (definitive/probable/possible/doubtful) — which drives both internal quality management and external FDA reporting obligations for fatal cases (SRC-032). Boards also test whether learners understand that not every DAT-positive, hemolysis-appearing picture is an alloimmune transfusion reaction: autoimmune hemolytic anemia also classically presents with a positive DAT (SRC-027), and thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura produces its own pentad of microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, neurologic signs, fever, and renal dysfunction that can superficially overlap with a hemolytic picture (SRC-023) — both are important differentials to keep in mind before anchoring on a transfusion reaction. Finally, the "dosage" phenomenon — antibodies (classically in the Rh, Duffy, Kidd, and MNS systems) reacting more strongly against homozygous-antigen red cells — helps explain why some hemolytic transfusion reactions do not present with the dramatic acute triad at all, and instead surface later as an unexplained failure to achieve the expected hemoglobin increment (SRC-024).
pitfalls: Jumping to a rare-antibody workup before completing the clerical/identity check: clerical error (patient, sample, or unit misidentification) is the leading preventable cause of acute intravascular (ABO-incompatible) hemolytic transfusion reactions, so identity verification is a required early step, not an afterthought (SRC-032).

Sending a clotted (serum) sample for the DAT instead of EDTA: clotted specimens can develop in-vitro complement attachment and produce a false-positive DAT, so specimen type affects interpretation of hemolysis workups (SRC-032).

Accepting a negative AHG-based result (DAT or antibody screen) without confirming that IgG-coated check cells reacted — a negative result is invalid if the check cells fail to react, since this is the only way to confirm the AHG reagent was added and functional (SRC-032).

Assuming all hemolytic transfusion reactions present with the dramatic acute triad of fever/chills/hemoglobinuria/"impending doom": antibodies exhibiting dosage (e.g., Kidd, Duffy, Rh, MNS) can be weak or undetectable on a pretransfusion screen and instead present later simply as a blunted-than-expected hemoglobin increment (SRC-024).

cases.title: Differential Diagnosis of Hemolytic Transfusion Reactions: AHTR vs DHTR vs Non-Hemolytic Mimickers
domains: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events
cases.clinical_setup: A 39-year-old woman with sickle cell disease, transfused on several prior admissions, is about 30 minutes into a red blood cell transfusion given for symptomatic anemia when she develops fever and hypotension. She is stabilized and the reaction is investigated. Later, after receiving a properly ABO/Rh-matched, crossmatch-compatible unit during a subsequent admission, she returns 8 days afterward with fatigue and an unexplained falling hemoglobin, without fever or hypotension.

findings.label: Temperature (Stage 1)
findings.value: 38.1°C, up from a pre-transfusion baseline of 37.0°C (rise <2°C)

findings.label: Repeat ABO/Rh typing, post-reaction sample (Stage 2)
findings.value: Patient types as group O; transfused unit was labeled group A

findings.label: Patient and unit blood cultures, sent to evaluate for bacterial contamination (Stage 2)
Hemoglobin, day 8 after subsequent (correctly matched) transfusion (Stage 3)
findings.value: 8.1 g/dL, down from a satisfactory post-transfusion value of 10.6 g/dL (adult reference range 12.0-16.0 g/dL)

findings.label: Eluate reactivity, day 8 (Stage 3)
findings.value: Reactive with Jk(a+) panel cells only; nonreactive with Jk(a-) cells and with patient's own (autologous) cells

decision_points.prompt: Thirty minutes into an RBC transfusion, this patient develops fever and hypotension. Before this is treated as a straightforward hemolytic transfusion reaction, which competing diagnoses must be actively excluded, and what findings argue against each?
options: TACO (circulatory overload)
TRALI (transfusion-related acute lung injury)
TAS (bacterial contamination/sepsis)
Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR)
decision_points.answer: TACO and TRALI are excluded on this presentation; TAS cannot be excluded and requires culture confirmation, while AHTR is pursued in parallel via clerical and serologic workup.
decision_points.rationale: TACO is characterized by hypertension, tachycardia, JVD, an S3 gallop, and a BNP/NT-proBNP rise of at least 1.5x baseline; this patient is hypotensive with no JVD/S3, arguing against TACO. TRALI requires severe hypoxemia (SpO2 <90%, PaO2/FiO2 <=300) and bilateral noncardiogenic infiltrates; her SpO2, PaO2/FiO2, and chest film are normal, arguing against TRALI. Bacterial contamination (TAS) classically causes a fever rise of >2°C with rigors and profound hypotension and, being a diagnosis that depends partly on culture results, cannot be excluded from the bedside picture alone even though her fever rise here is milder than that threshold; patient and unit cultures must still be sent.

decision_points.prompt: The post-reaction sample shows the patient typing as group O, but the transfused unit was labeled group A, and the post-reaction DAT is newly positive. What is the diagnosis, and what confirms it?
options: AHTR from ABO incompatibility (clerical/identification error)
Febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reaction
TACO
TRALI
decision_points.answer: Acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR) due to ABO-incompatible transfusion from a bedside/identification (clerical) error.
decision_points.rationale: AHTR is the immune, acute (<24 hour) reaction classified as ABO-mediated. The discrepancy between the patient's confirmed group-O type and the group-A labeled unit demonstrates a clerical/identification error, and the newly positive DAT reflects immune-mediated destruction of the incompatible transfused cells. This DAT-centered confirmation mirrors the general immunohematologic approach to transfusion-reaction workup, where DAT and downstream elution/antibody identification are used to establish the cause of hemolysis.

decision_points.prompt: After recovering and later receiving a properly ABO/Rh-matched, crossmatch-compatible unit, the patient returns 8 days later with fatigue and a falling hemoglobin, without fever or hypotension. Her antibody screen, negative immediately pretransfusion, is now positive, and her DAT is positive. How do you differentiate a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction from a warm autoimmune process here, and which antibody system is classically implicated?
options: Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction (anamnestic anti-Jka)
Warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia (WAIHA) unmasked around the time of transfusion
Ongoing occult hemorrhage
A second acute hemolytic transfusion reaction
decision_points.answer: Delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction (DHTR) from an anamnestic secondary immune response, classically implicated with Kidd (Jka) antibodies.
decision_points.rationale: A newly positive antibody screen or DAT appearing after transfusion indicates a secondary (anamnestic) immune response, which is the defining serologic feature of DHTR. Kidd system antibodies are specifically associated with rapid titer decline between exposures and with delayed hemolytic transfusion reactions, explaining why the immediate pretransfusion screen was negative despite presumed prior sensitization. The eluate reacting specifically with Jk(a+) cells and not with the patient's own autologous cells argues against the panreactive pattern that is the hallmark eluate finding in warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia, so this cell-specific reactivity is what distinguishes an alloantibody-driven DHTR from an autoimmune process.

cases.answer_key: Stage 1 (intra-transfusion fever + hypotension): TACO is excluded by the absence of hypertension, JVD/S3, and BNP rise; TRALI is excluded by normal SpO2, normal PaO2/FiO2, and a clear chest film; TAS cannot be excluded on presentation alone (fever rise is <2°C here, which argues against it, but patient/unit cultures must still be sent) — AHTR and TAS remain the leading considerations pending clerical and serologic workup. Stage 2 (clerical + serologic confirmation): a repeat-sample ABO discrepancy (patient O, unit labeled A) plus a newly positive post-reaction DAT confirms an acute hemolytic transfusion reaction (AHTR) from ABO incompatibility due to a bedside/identification (clerical) error — stop the transfusion, verify identification, and proceed with the standard immunohematologic workup. Stage 3 (delayed presentation after a correctly matched unit): an unexplained hemoglobin drop 8 days later, with a newly positive antibody screen/DAT (negative pretransfusion) and an eluate reactive specifically with Jk(a+) cells (not panreactive/not reactive with autologous cells), confirms a delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction (DHTR) from an anamnestic anti-Jka response rather than a warm autoimmune hemolytic process.
cases.teaching_explanation: Differential diagnosis of hemolytic transfusion reactions is built on timing, hemodynamics, and serology working together. Acutely, non-hemolytic mimickers are excluded first: TACO by its hypertensive, volume-overloaded picture with JVD/S3 and a rising BNP, and TRALI by its hypoxemic picture with bilateral noncardiogenic infiltrates; bacterial contamination (TAS) is a fever-and-hypotension mimicker that cannot be excluded without cultures, since its hallmark is an even larger fever spike with rigors and profound hypotension. Once these are excluded or pending, AHTR is confirmed the same way any unexplained incompatibility or hemolytic picture is worked up: a clerical/ABO discrepancy plus a newly positive DAT. Delayed reactions look different by design — they surface more than 24 hours out, are driven by anamnestic alloantibody responses (classically Kidd, which evanesces below detectable levels between exposures), and are confirmed serologically by a newly positive screen/DAT together with an eluate that shows specific, not panreactive, reactivity. That same panreactive-versus-specific eluate distinction is also how a true DHTR is separated from a warm autoimmune hemolytic process uncovered around the time of transfusion.
pitfalls: Attributing fever plus hypotension during a transfusion solely to sepsis or TRALI without checking for the hypertensive/volume-overload signs of TACO or the hypoxemia/infiltrates of TRALI can delay recognizing ABO-incompatible AHTR, which shares the febrile, hypotensive presentation but is confirmed by an ABO/clerical discrepancy plus a positive DAT.

A negative pretransfusion antibody screen does not exclude prior alloimmunization: Kidd (Jk) antibodies are notorious for evanescing below detectable titers between exposures, then reappearing rapidly on re-exposure to cause DHTR — a newly positive screen or DAT after transfusion should always trigger a secondary-response workup.

Autoadsorption to separate an autoantibody from a possible underlying alloantibody is invalid if the patient has been transfused within the last 3 months, since as little as 10% donor cells in circulation can adsorb out a clinically significant alloantibody and produce a false-negative screen; differential (allogeneic) adsorption must be used instead.

A panreactive eluate (reactive with all panel cells, including the patient's own autologous cells) points toward a warm autoantibody rather than a discrete alloantibody; failing to distinguish panreactivity from cell-specific reactivity (e.g., Jk(a+)-restricted) risks misclassifying a DHTR as an autoimmune process, or vice versa.

cases.title: Anemia in an AML Induction Patient: Transfusion Threshold, CMV-Safe Components, Irradiation, and the Storage Lesion
cases.clinical_setup: A 52-year-old woman with newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia (AML) is 5 days into cytarabine/anthracycline induction chemotherapy on the inpatient oncology service. She is CMV-seronegative and is a planned candidate for allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) once she achieves remission. Nursing reports new exertional dyspnea when she walks to the bathroom, though she remains hemodynamically stable at rest. Her most recent CBC shows a falling hemoglobin during her expected chemotherapy-induced pancytopenic nadir. The oncology team consults the transfusion medicine service for anemia and blood component support.

findings.label: Transplant plan
findings.value: CMV-seronegative allogeneic HSCT candidate, pending remission

findings.label: Post-transfusion hemoglobin (after 2 units leukoreduced, irradiated RBCs)
2,3-DPG level in stored donor RBC unit

decision_points.prompt: Given her Hgb of 6.8 g/dL with new exertional dyspnea but hemodynamic stability at rest (no tachycardia, hypotension, or chest pain), what transfusion threshold and physiologic reasoning should guide the decision to transfuse RBCs now?
options: Transfuse immediately at any Hgb decline regardless of symptoms or threshold
Withhold transfusion until Hgb falls below 5 g/dL
Apply the Hgb <7 g/dL threshold used in blood utilization review criteria for stable patients; transfuse now since she has crossed it and is symptomatic
Defer transfusion indefinitely because compensatory mechanisms fully normalize oxygen delivery in anemia
decision_points.answer: Apply the Hgb <7 g/dL threshold used in blood utilization review criteria for stable patients; transfuse now since she has crossed it and is symptomatic
decision_points.rationale: Blood utilization audit/review criteria commonly use a threshold of Hgb <7 g/dL for stable patients to judge appropriateness of RBC transfusion. Compensatory mechanisms for anemia (increased cardiac output, increased RBC 2,3-DPG shifting the oxygen dissociation curve right to favor tissue O2 release, and redistribution of blood flow to vital organs) allow tolerance of anemia up to a point, but once the threshold is crossed and symptoms such as exertional dyspnea appear, transfusion is indicated even though the patient is stable at rest.

decision_points.prompt: She is CMV-seronegative and being prepared for allogeneic HSCT. What RBC product characteristic should be specified to mitigate transfusion-transmitted CMV risk?
options: No CMV mitigation is needed since she already has leukemia
Prestorage-leukoreduced ('CMV-safe') RBC units are appropriate and have nearly replaced CMV-seronegative units in many institutions
Only true CMV-seronegative donor units may ever be used for any CMV-seronegative recipient
CMV mitigation is only relevant for platelet transfusions, not RBCs
decision_points.answer: Prestorage-leukoreduced ('CMV-safe') RBC units are appropriate and have nearly replaced CMV-seronegative units in many institutions
decision_points.rationale: CMV is a latent virus carried within monocytes and granulocytes, and mitigation is critical for at-risk populations including seronegative HSCT recipients. Prestorage leukocyte reduction (to <5x10^6 WBCs) effectively removes the CMV-carrying leukocytes; universal leukoreduction is considered 'CMV-safe' and has nearly replaced the use of seronegative units in many institutions, with true CMV-seronegative units reserved for highly specific protocols where 'safe' is not deemed sufficient.

decision_points.prompt: Beyond CMV mitigation, does this patient's cellular blood component order need any additional processing given her clinical status, and why?
options: No additional processing is required beyond leukoreduction
The units should be irradiated to inactivate donor T lymphocytes and prevent TA-GVHD
The units should be washed to remove plasma proteins only
The units should undergo pathogen inactivation with amotosalen/UVA instead of irradiation
decision_points.answer: The units should be irradiated to inactivate donor T lymphocytes and prevent TA-GVHD
decision_points.rationale: Transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD) is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components, which inactivates donor T lymphocytes. A patient undergoing intensive AML induction chemotherapy and preparing for allogeneic HSCT is profoundly immunocompromised and cannot reject engrafting donor lymphocytes, making irradiated cellular components the appropriate order alongside leukoreduction.

decision_points.prompt: After transfusion of 2 units of leukoreduced, irradiated RBCs her hemoglobin appropriately rises to 8.5 g/dL, but she still reports fatigue with minimal exertion in the first few hours afterward. What storage-related physiologic property of the transfused units best explains why immediate tissue oxygen delivery may lag behind the hemoglobin rise?
options: Stored RBC units have increased 2,3-DPG, shifting the curve right and impairing O2 release
Stored RBC units have decreased 2,3-DPG, left-shifting the oxygen dissociation curve so hemoglobin holds oxygen more tightly and releases it less readily to tissue
Stored units are hyperoxygenated and cannot bind additional oxygen
The hemoglobin rise indicates a transfusion reaction requiring no physiologic explanation
decision_points.answer: Stored RBC units have decreased 2,3-DPG, left-shifting the oxygen dissociation curve so hemoglobin holds oxygen more tightly and releases it less readily to tissue
decision_points.rationale: A leftward shift of the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve is caused by decreased 2,3-DPG (a recognized feature of the RBC storage lesion), along with decreased CO2, decreased temperature, or increased pH. A left-shifted curve means hemoglobin holds oxygen more tightly, potentially impairing tissue oxygen delivery immediately after transfusion even though the hemoglobin concentration itself has normalized.

cases.answer_key: Transfuse RBCs using the Hgb <7 g/dL restrictive threshold appropriate for a hemodynamically stable but now-symptomatic oncology patient; select prestorage-leukoreduced ("CMV-safe") RBC units given her CMV-seronegative, pre-allogeneic-HSCT status (rather than requiring true CMV-seronegative units); additionally irradiate all cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes and prevent TA-GVHD given her profound treatment-related immunosuppression; and recognize that early post-transfusion fatigue can reflect the transient left-shifted oxyhemoglobin curve from storage-lesion 2,3-DPG depletion rather than an inadequate or failed transfusion.
cases.teaching_explanation: Anemia in oncology patients requires integrating red cell physiology with transfusion-safety decision-making rather than treating transfusion as a simple numbers exercise. Compensatory mechanisms -- increased cardiac output, increased 2,3-DPG, and redistribution of blood flow -- allow patients to tolerate progressive anemia, but restrictive institutional review criteria (Hgb <7 g/dL for stable patients) mark the point at which transfusion becomes the appropriate intervention rather than an optional one. Because many oncology patients are pre-HSCT or otherwise severely immunocompromised, component selection must also address CMV transmission risk (prestorage leukoreduction as the modern 'CMV-safe' standard, with true seronegative units reserved for narrower indications) and TA-GVHD risk (prevented by gamma irradiation, which inactivates donor T lymphocytes that could otherwise engraft in a host unable to mount a rejection response). Finally, the RBC storage lesion -- specifically depletion of 2,3-DPG -- left-shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve in stored units, so a normalized hemoglobin value does not guarantee immediate normalization of tissue oxygen delivery.
pitfalls: Treating the Hgb <7 g/dL figure as a rigid universal transfusion mandate rather than an audit/review criterion for stable patients -- symptoms and hemodynamic status still drive the actual clinical decision.

Assuming true CMV-seronegative donor units are mandatory for every CMV-seronegative recipient -- prestorage leukoreduction is considered 'CMV-safe' and has nearly replaced seronegative units in many institutions except in highly specific protocols.
Forgetting to order irradiated cellular components for severely immunocompromised oncology/HSCT patients, risking TA-GVHD from viable donor T lymphocytes that the host cannot reject.

Expecting immediate, full restoration of tissue oxygen delivery the moment hemoglobin normalizes post-transfusion, without accounting for the left-shifted oxyhemoglobin curve caused by storage-lesion 2,3-DPG depletion.

source_references.supports: Hgb <7 g/dL is used as a blood utilization review/audit criterion for judging RBC transfusion appropriateness in stable patients

source_references.supports: Compensatory mechanisms for anemia include increased cardiac output and increased 2,3-DPG (right-shifting the curve); stored blood has decreased 2,3-DPG, left-shifting the oxygen dissociation curve and potentially impairing tissue oxygen delivery

source_references.supports: Prestorage leukoreduction to <5x10^6 WBCs is considered 'CMV-safe' and has nearly replaced CMV-seronegative units in many institutions; seronegative units are reserved for highly specific protocols; seronegative HSCT recipients are a key at-risk population for CMV mitigation

source_references.locator: Domain V - Hazards of Transfusion
source_references.supports: TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular blood components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes

source_references.locator: Domain IX - Administration and Laboratory Management
source_references.supports: Patient Blood Management is described as a multidisciplinary approach to optimizing care of patients who may need transfusion, framing the overall anemia-management approach in the case

cases.title: Immune Platelet Transfusion Refractoriness: CCI, HLA Class I Alloantibodies, and Escalation to HPA Testing
cases.clinical_setup: A 34-year-old woman (G2P2, two prior term pregnancies) with newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia is undergoing induction chemotherapy and receiving prophylactic platelet support for severe thrombocytopenia. Despite receiving ABO-identical single-donor apheresis platelet units on consecutive days, she shows minimal rise in platelet count and develops scattered petechiae. She is intermittently febrile but hemodynamically stable, with no organomegaly on exam. The transfusion service is asked to determine whether she has developed platelet transfusion refractoriness and, if so, whether it is immune-mediated.

findings.label: Platelet count, 1 hour post-transfusion (unit 1; dose 3.0 ×10^11 platelets; BSA 1.8 m²)
Platelet count, 1 hour post-transfusion (unit 2; same dose/BSA)
Calculated 1-hour CCI, transfusion 2 (second consecutive low value)
ABO type: patient and platelet units
findings.value: Group A patient; both apheresis units ABO-identical (Group A)

findings.label: Coagulation panel (PT/PTT/fibrinogen/D-dimer)
findings.value: Within reference range, no DIC pattern

findings.label: Obstetric history
findings.value: G2P2, two prior term pregnancies (HLA-sensitizing exposure)

findings.label: HLA antibody screen (solid-phase multiantigen bead/flow cytometric assay)
findings.value: Positive; broad reactivity against HLA-A and -B loci; elevated calculated PRA (cPRA)

findings.label: 1-hour CCI after trial of HLA-matched (zero HLA-A/-B mismatch) apheresis unit

decision_points.prompt: Using the pre- and 1-hour post-transfusion counts, BSA (1.8 m²), and platelet dose (3.0 ×10^11) from transfusion 1, calculate the CCI and state what a single low value like this suggests about the etiology of her poor increment.
decision_points.answer: 1-hour CCI = (5,000/µL increment × 1.8 m²) / 3.0 (×10^11) = 3,000/µL. Because immune-mediated refractoriness is suggested by a 1-hour CCI <5,000–7,500/µL, this single low value raises concern for immune destruction, but the diagnostic criterion requires demonstrating this pattern on two consecutive transfusions before proceeding further.
decision_points.rationale: SRC-026 defines the CCI formula (increment × BSA / platelets transfused in 10^11 units) and states that a 1-hour CCI <5,000–7,500/µL on two consecutive transfusions suggests immune refractoriness.

decision_points.prompt: The following day's transfusion again yields a low 1-hour CCI (~3,300/µL), satisfying the two-consecutive-transfusion criterion. Blood cultures are negative, coagulation studies show no DIC pattern, there is no splenomegaly, and both units were ABO-identical. What has this evaluation accomplished before an HLA antibody workup is ordered?
decision_points.answer: It has excluded the major nonimmune causes of refractoriness (sepsis/DIC, splenomegaly, medication effect) that account for roughly 80% of cases, and it has ruled out ABO mismatch as a confounder of a poor increment — both necessary before attributing the poor CCI to alloimmunization.
decision_points.rationale: SRC-026 notes that approximately 80% of refractoriness is nonimmune (sepsis, DIC, splenomegaly, medications), and that ABO-mismatched platelets (e.g., Group A platelets to a Group O recipient) can also blunt increments and should be checked before concluding immune refractoriness.

decision_points.prompt: With immune refractoriness now supported, what test should be ordered, what antigens does it target, and how should a positive HLA Class II result on the same panel be interpreted?
decision_points.answer: Order HLA antibody screening by a solid-phase (multiantigen bead/flow cytometric) assay to determine the patient's panel-reactive antibody (PRA/cPRA). The primary target in immune platelet refractoriness is HLA Class I (HLA-A and -B), the antigens expressed on the platelet surface. Platelets do not express HLA Class II, so a positive Class II result reflects sensitization to contaminating leukocytes in prior products rather than antibody directed at the platelets themselves. Her obstetric history (G2P2) is clinically relevant, as pregnancy is the most significant risk factor for primary HLA sensitization.
decision_points.rationale: SRC-026 describes HLA screening via multiantigen-coated bead flow cytometry to determine PRA/cPRA, identifies HLA Class I (A, B) as the primary immune target, flags Class II positivity as a leukocyte-sensitization distractor, and names pregnancy as the leading HLA-sensitizing risk factor. SRC-028 and SRC-010 corroborate that platelet/nucleated-cell expression is restricted to Class I (A, B, C) while Class II is restricted to antigen-presenting cells.

decision_points.prompt: The HLA screen shows broad HLA-A/-B reactivity with elevated cPRA. What platelet-selection strategy should be used, what safety step is mandatory for the selected product, and what should be done because she remains refractory despite a trial of HLA-matched platelets?
decision_points.answer: Select platelets via HLA matching (identical or zero-mismatch HLA-A/-B donors) or, as an equally effective and often faster alternative, HLA-antibody avoidance using a virtual crossmatch (ASP method — selecting donors lacking antigens corresponding to her antibody specificities) or physical platelet crossmatching (solid-phase red cell adherence assay). Any HLA-selected or crossmatched unit must be irradiated, because the high degree of donor-recipient matching increases the risk of donor T-cell engraftment and TA-GVHD. Since her CCI remains low despite an HLA-matched unit, proceed to platelet-specific (HPA) antibody testing; anti-HPA-1a is identified, so she now requires HPA antigen-negative platelets.
decision_points.rationale: SRC-026 lists HLA-matched platelets, HLA-antibody avoidance via virtual crossmatch (ASP, 'as effective as matching'), and SPRCA platelet crossmatching as management options; mandates irradiation of HLA-selected/crossmatched platelets to prevent TA-GVHD; and states that antigen-negative (HPA) selection is required if refractoriness persists with HLA-compatible units and a platelet-specific antibody such as anti-HPA-1a is identified. SRC-006 adds that platelets carry ABO, HLA Class I, and HPA antigen systems, and that HPA antibodies are also relevant to FNAIT and post-transfusion purpura.

cases.answer_key: Two consecutive 1-hour CCIs <5,000–7,500/µL, obtained only after excluding the ~80% nonimmune causes of refractoriness (sepsis/DIC, splenomegaly, medication effect) and confirming ABO-identical product, establish immune platelet refractoriness. Solid-phase HLA antibody screening demonstrates HLA Class I (A/B) reactivity with an elevated cPRA — consistent with prior pregnancy as her primary HLA-sensitizing exposure — while a negative Class II result confirms the antibodies are platelet-directed rather than reflecting leukocyte sensitization. Management proceeds to HLA-matched platelets or, as an equally effective (and often faster) alternative, antibody-specificity-based avoidance via virtual crossmatch (ASP method) or physical platelet crossmatching (SPRCA); any HLA-selected/crossmatched product must be irradiated to prevent TA-GVHD. Because refractoriness persists despite HLA-compatible platelets, the workup escalates to platelet-specific (HPA) antibody testing, anti-HPA-1a is identified, and HPA antigen-negative platelets are required.
cases.teaching_explanation: Immune-mediated platelet destruction spans a spectrum from autoimmune ITP to alloimmune transfusion refractoriness, and the source curriculum groups HLA/HPA antibody assays under this Immune Thrombocytopenia node because they are the workhorse tests for investigating platelet destruction in multiply transfused patients. The diagnostic logic is kinetic before it is serologic: the 1-hour CCI (increment × BSA / platelets transfused in 10^11) separates immune destruction (poor 1-hour recovery) from nonimmune consumption (acceptable 1-hour recovery with decay by 18–24 hours), and a threshold of <5,000–7,500/µL on two consecutive transfusions is the trigger for an immune workup — but only after the ~80% of nonimmune causes and ABO mismatch are excluded. Serologic confirmation targets HLA Class I (A, B), the only HLA class platelets express; a positive Class II result is a leukocyte-sensitization distractor, not evidence of platelet-directed disease. Management is stepwise: HLA-matched or antibody-avoidance (virtual crossmatch/ASP) or crossmatched platelets first, always irradiated because close HLA matching raises TA-GVHD risk, and HPA antigen-negative platelets reserved for refractoriness that persists despite HLA-compatible product and a demonstrated platelet-specific antibody (e.g., anti-HPA-1a).
pitfalls: Ordering an HLA antibody workup before excluding the ~80% of refractoriness cases that are nonimmune (sepsis/DIC, splenomegaly, medications) — the CCI pattern alone does not replace this clinical exclusion.

Misreading a positive HLA Class II antibody as evidence of platelet-directed alloimmunization — platelets do not express Class II, so a positive result reflects sensitization to contaminating leukocytes, a classic board distractor.

Forgetting that HLA-matched, ASP-selected, or crossmatched platelet units still require irradiation, since the closer donor-recipient HLA match paradoxically raises the risk of donor T-cell engraftment and TA-GVHD.

Confusing the timing pattern (a good 1-hour count with a poor 24-hour count indicates nonimmune consumption, not HLA-antibody-mediated destruction) and failing to confirm ABO-identical/compatible product before attributing a poor increment to alloimmunization.

source_references.supports: Defines refractoriness, the 80%/20% nonimmune/immune split, HLA Class I (A/B) as the primary immune target, the CCI formula and its <5,000–7,500/µL threshold on two consecutive transfusions, HLA screening via multiantigen bead flow cytometry (PRA/cPRA), management options (HLA-matched, ASP virtual crossmatch, SPRCA crossmatch, HPA antigen-negative escalation), mandatory irradiation of HLA-selected

Confirms HLA Class I (A, B, C) expression on platelets and nucleated cells versus Class II restriction to antigen-presenting cells, and defines the virtual crossmatch as comparing the recipient's known HLA antibody profile against the donor's HLA type.

States that ITP-spectrum platelet destruction is investigated with HLA and HPA antibody assays, and that MHC Class I is on nearly all nucleated cells/platelets while Class II is restricted to antigen-presenting cells.

Establishes that platelets carry ABO, HLA Class I (A, B), and HPA antigen systems; that HPA antibodies (e.g., anti-HPA-1a) are also relevant to FNAIT and post-transfusion purpura; and that genotyping supplements serologic HPA typing when reagents are unavailable or antibody is weak.
