cases.title: New Thrombocytopenia on Heparin: Immune Thrombocytopenia (HIT/ITP) vs. TTP and Why TPE Is Not Reflexive
cases.clinical_setup: A 64-year-old man is postoperative day 8 after major surgery and has been maintained on a continuous unfractionated heparin infusion for venous thromboembolism prophylaxis. His platelet count, previously stable, has fallen, prompting an urgent hematology/transfusion medicine consult. There is no bleeding. The team must reason through whether this represents an antibody-mediated (immune) thrombocytopenic process — with heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) in the differential — versus thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP), and whether therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) should be started empirically while confirmatory testing is pending.

findings.label: Platelet count trend
findings.value: 142,000/µL on admission → 58,000/µL by hospital day 8, on ongoing unfractionated heparin infusion

findings.label: Renal function (creatinine)
findings.value: Within normal limits, unchanged from baseline

findings.label: Hemolysis markers / peripheral smear
findings.value: No schistocytosis; LDH and haptoglobin unremarkable

findings.label: TTP pentad criteria met
findings.value: 1 of 5 (thrombocytopenia only); microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, neurologic signs, fever, and renal dysfunction all absent

decision_points.prompt: A hospitalized patient on heparin develops new, unexplained thrombocytopenia. What is the correct first step in clinical reasoning?
options: Empirically begin therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) given any hospitalized patient with new thrombocytopenia
Characterize the thrombocytopenia by underlying mechanism (immune vs. nonimmune) before committing to a specific treatment pathway
Assume this is TRALI or TACO and evaluate with BNP/NT-proBNP and chest imaging
No further workup is needed since an isolated platelet drop without bleeding is expected on heparin
decision_points.answer: Characterize the thrombocytopenia by underlying mechanism (immune vs. nonimmune) before committing to a specific treatment pathway
decision_points.rationale: The approved curriculum organizes hematologic/transfusion-related processes by underlying pathophysiologic mechanism: SRC-045 frames transfusion reaction classification explicitly around Immune vs. Nonimmune mechanism, and SRC-010 places Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT) within the 'Immune Thrombocytopenia' clinical-practice subdomain, paired with ITP. Before a specific management pathway (e.g., TTP-directed ADAMTS13/TPE workup, or an immune-thrombocytopenia antibody-assay workup) can be chosen, the mechanism must be established rather than assumed.

decision_points.prompt: The patient is afebrile, has a nonfocal neurologic exam, normal renal function, and no schistocytosis or hemolysis markers -- only isolated thrombocytopenia. How should this shape the differential relative to a TTP-suspected patient?
options: Because the patient does not exhibit the TTP pentad (MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neurologic signs, fever, renal dysfunction), the TTP-specific pathway (PLASMIC score / ADAMTS13-directed management) is lower yield; proceed down the immune thrombocytopenia (ITP/HIT) antibody-assay pathway instead
The absence of fever excludes any immune mechanism entirely
Any thrombocytopenia in a hospitalized patient should be presumed due to TTP until ADAMTS13 activity returns
The PLASMIC score cannot inform reasoning until the ADAMTS13 result is available, so no interim differential is possible
decision_points.answer: Because the patient does not exhibit the TTP pentad (MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neurologic signs, fever, renal dysfunction), the TTP-specific pathway (PLASMIC score / ADAMTS13-directed management) is lower yield; proceed down the immune thrombocytopenia (ITP/HIT) antibody-assay pathway instead
decision_points.rationale: SRC-023 defines the TTP pentad as microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (MAHA), thrombocytopenia, neurologic signs, fever, and renal dysfunction, and describes the PLASMIC score as a tool used to discriminate TTP from other microangiopathies specifically while awaiting ADAMTS13 results -- i.e., interim reasoning before ADAMTS13 returns is exactly the intended use case. A patient with isolated thrombocytopenia and none of the other pentad features is a poor fit for the TTP pathway, supporting evaluation within the curriculum's immune thrombocytopenia (ITP/HIT) category instead (SRC-010).

decision_points.prompt: Should therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) be started empirically for this heparin-associated thrombocytopenia while confirmatory antibody testing is pending?
options: Yes -- TPE removes pathogenic antibodies regardless of the specific disease mechanism, so it is reasonable to start empirically

No -- the approved apheresis source lists ASFA Category I (accepted, first-line) indications as conditions such as TTP and Guillain-Barré syndrome; it does not establish immune thrombocytopenia (ITP/HIT) as a TPE indication, and TPE's proven TTP mechanism (removing the ADAMTS13 inhibitor/ultra-large vWF multimers while replacing ADAMTS13) does not apply here
Yes -- because a 1.0 plasma-volume exchange removes roughly 63-70% of any intravascular substance, this alone justifies use in any antibody-mediated cytopenia
No -- TPE is absolutely contraindicated in all thrombocytopenic patients regardless of etiology
decision_points.answer: No -- the approved apheresis source lists ASFA Category I (accepted, first-line) indications as conditions such as TTP and Guillain-Barré syndrome; it does not establish immune thrombocytopenia (ITP/HIT) as a TPE indication, and TPE's proven TTP mechanism (removing the ADAMTS13 inhibitor/ultra-large vWF multimers while replacing ADAMTS13) does not apply here
decision_points.rationale: SRC-023 states ASFA Category I disorders are those for which apheresis is accepted as first-line therapy, giving TTP and Guillain-Barré syndrome as examples; it does not list HIT or ITP among these. The same source explains TPE is first-line for TTP specifically because it removes the ADAMTS13 inhibitor and ultra-large vWF multimers while replacing the deficient protease -- a mechanism tied to TTP pathophysiology, not to an antibody-mediated platelet disorder in general. The fact that a 1.0-PV exchange removes ~63-70% of an intravascular substance (SRC-023) describes removal kinetics, not clinical indication, and is a common board distractor.

decision_points.prompt: What laboratory approach does the approved curriculum indicate for confirming an immune thrombocytopenia diagnosis such as HIT, and what is a key limitation of these sources on this specific point?
options: The curriculum states antibody-based assays (explicitly, 'HLA and HPA antibody assays' for ITP investigation) are the relevant category of laboratory testing for the paired immune thrombocytopenia entities (ITP/HIT); however, the approved sources do not name the specific confirmatory assay(s) used for HIT itself
The curriculum provides a complete antigen/functional HIT assay algorithm with specific numeric cutoffs
No laboratory testing approach is described anywhere in the curriculum for immune thrombocytopenias
The BNP/NT-proBNP ≥1.5x criterion used to support a TACO diagnosis also applies to confirming HIT
decision_points.answer: The curriculum states antibody-based assays (explicitly, 'HLA and HPA antibody assays' for ITP investigation) are the relevant category of laboratory testing for the paired immune thrombocytopenia entities (ITP/HIT); however, the approved sources do not name the specific confirmatory assay(s) used for HIT itself
decision_points.rationale: SRC-010 states that 'ITP involves platelet destruction where HLA and HPA antibody assays are critical for investigation' and separately groups 'classification and pathogenesis of Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP) and Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT)' together as a Domain I high-yield concept. This establishes that the diagnostic paradigm for this category is antibody-based laboratory testing rather than the pentad/PLASMIC/ADAMTS13/TPE pathway used for TTP -- but the approved source set does not go on to name the specific confirmatory assay(s) used for HIT, which is a genuine gap in this particular source set that the learner should recognize rather than fill in with unverified outside content. The BNP/NT-proBNP ≥1.5x and PaO2/FiO2 ≤300 criteria (SRC-050) are specific to TACO/TRALI diagnosis, not to thrombocytopenia workups, and are a distractor here.

cases.answer_key: New thrombocytopenia in a hospitalized patient on heparin must first be classified by mechanism (immune vs. nonimmune) rather than assigned a treatment reflexively. Because this patient lacks the TTP pentad (MAHA, neurologic signs, fever, renal dysfunction) and the clinical picture does not fit the ADAMTS13/PLASMIC-driven TTP pathway, the case is worked up as an immune-mediated thrombocytopenia in the curriculum's paired ITP/HIT category, using antibody-based laboratory assays — the investigative modality the approved curriculum specifies for that category — rather than starting therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE). TPE is not initiated empirically: the approved apheresis source restricts ASFA Category I (accepted, first-line) apheresis indications to conditions such as TTP and Guillain-Barré syndrome and does not establish immune thrombocytopenia (ITP/HIT) as a TPE indication, and TPE's proven TTP-specific mechanism (removing the ADAMTS13 inhibitor and ultra-large vWF multimers while replacing ADAMTS13) does not carry over to an antibody-mediated platelet disorder outside that mechanism.
cases.teaching_explanation: The approved board curriculum (SRC-010) groups Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT) with Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP) under the Clinical Practice domain, framing both as immune/antibody-mediated platelet destruction whose investigation centers on antibody-based assays (stated explicitly for ITP). This case teaches the surrounding clinical reasoning that the approved sources do support well: (1) hematologic and transfusion-related processes should be classified by mechanism -- immune vs. nonimmune -- before a management pathway is chosen (SRC-045); (2) thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) is a distinct, richly source-documented entity with its own pentad (MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neurologic signs, fever, renal dysfunction), the PLASMIC score, ADAMTS13-directed pathophysiology, and first-line therapeutic plasma exchange (SRC-023); and (3) apheresis is not a generic antibody-removal tool to be reached for whenever an antibody-mediated process is suspected -- ASFA Category I (first-line, accepted) indications are specific (e.g., TTP, Guillain-Barré syndrome), and the source materials explicitly warn about over-extending apheresis to conditions where it is ineffective or unproven (SRC-023's Category IV distractor teaching pearl). Honesty about source limits is itself part of the lesson: these four approved sources establish that HIT exists as a named, antibody-mediated immune thrombocytopenia paired with ITP, but they do not provide the specific confirmatory laboratory assay(s) for HIT (e.g., antigen- or function-based platelet-antibody testing). A learner using only this source set should be able to correctly triage the case away from TTP/apheresis and toward an antibody-assay-based ITP/HIT pathway, while recognizing that assay-level HIT detail would require material beyond these four documents.
pitfalls: Assuming any antibody-mediated cytopenia automatically qualifies for therapeutic plasma exchange -- the ASFA Category I list is specific (e.g., TTP, Guillain-Barré syndrome), not an open-ended category for 'antibodies are pathogenic, so remove them.'

Misapplying pulmonary-reaction-specific criteria (BNP/NT-proBNP ≥1.5x for TACO, PaO2/FiO2 ≤300 for TRALI/TACO hypoxemia) to an unrelated thrombocytopenia workup -- these thresholds are defined in the sources strictly for pulmonary transfusion reactions.

Treating the fact that a 1.0-plasma-volume exchange removes ~63-70% of an intravascular substance as proof of clinical efficacy or indication, rather than recognizing it as a description of extracorporeal removal kinetics that applies regardless of whether treatment is actually indicated.

Assuming the approved source set provides a full HIT confirmatory-assay algorithm -- it only establishes HIT's classification alongside ITP as an immune thrombocytopenia and the general antibody-assay paradigm (stated for ITP); board-level HIT assay specifics are not present in these four sources and should not be presented as if they were.

source_references.locator: Domain I: Clinical Practice, High-Yield Facts
source_references.supports: Groups HIT with ITP under the Clinical Practice / Immune Thrombocytopenia subdomain and states that antibody-based assays (HLA and HPA) are critical for investigating this category (stated for ITP).

source_references.locator: Definitions; Diagnostic Approach; Management; Common Board Traps; Teaching Pearls
source_references.supports: Defines the TTP pentad, the PLASMIC score's role in differentiating TTP while awaiting ADAMTS13, the ADAMTS13-based mechanism making TPE first-line for TTP, ASFA Category I examples (TTP, Guillain-Barré), and the plasma-volume removal kinetics (~63-70% per 1.0 PV exchange) used as a distractor.

source_references.locator: Core Concepts, Classification
source_references.supports: Establishes the general principle that hematologic/transfusion-related reactions are classified by mechanism (Immune vs. Nonimmune) before a management pathway is assigned.

source_references.locator: Laboratory Findings section
source_references.supports: Defines the BNP/NT-proBNP ≥1.5x and PaO2/FiO2 ≤300 criteria specific to TACO/TRALI, used in this case as a distractor to show these thresholds do not apply to a thrombocytopenia workup.

review.notes: Verified all four sources directly. Quoted material is generally accurate and traceable — the TTP pentad, PLASMIC score usage, ASFA Category I examples (TTP, GBS), the ADAMTS13-inhibitor/ultra-large vWF mechanism, the 1.0-PV/63-70% removal distractor, the SRC-045 Immune vs. Nonimmune classification language, the HLA/HPA-for-ITP quote, and the TACO/TRALI BNP and PaO2/FiO2 criteria all match the source text closely (near-verbatim in several places). None of the four decision-point answer keys is factually wrong given the sources — each selected option is the best-supported choice among its distr

cases.title: Anemia of Prematurity: The Blunted Erythropoietin Response in a Very Preterm Neonate
cases.clinical_setup: A former 28-week-gestation infant (birth weight 1100 g), now day of life 21 in the NICU (postmenstrual age ~31 weeks), develops new tachycardia, tachypnea, and poor weight gain over several days, with an increasing supplemental oxygen requirement. The infant has undergone numerous heel-stick and venous draws for routine NICU monitoring but has no history of overt bleeding. The prenatal maternal antibody screen and the infant's initial newborn antibody screen were both negative.

findings.label: Absolute reticulocyte count
findings.value: Low, inappropriately reduced for the degree of anemia

findings.label: Serum erythropoietin (if measured)
findings.value: Inappropriately low/blunted relative to the degree of anemia

decision_points.prompt: The infant is anemic and symptomatic (tachycardia, tachypnea, poor weight gain, rising oxygen requirement), yet the reticulocyte count remains inappropriately low rather than showing a robust regenerative response. What is the underlying physiologic explanation for this hyporegenerative pattern in a neonate this age?
options: Bone marrow aplasia secondary to prematurity
Blunted erythropoietin (EPO) response to anemia, a physiologic feature of neonates less than 4 months of age
Ongoing occult hemolysis consuming reticulocytes as fast as they are produced
Folate deficiency suppressing marrow erythropoiesis
decision_points.answer: Blunted erythropoietin (EPO) response to anemia, a physiologic feature of neonates less than 4 months of age
decision_points.rationale: Red cell production is regulated by erythropoietin (SRC-024). Neonates under 4 months have immature organ systems, small blood volumes (~85-100 mL/kg), and a blunted EPO response to anemia (SRC-017, SRC-025), so the marrow signal that would normally drive reticulocytosis is attenuated, producing a hyporegenerative anemia despite clinical symptoms of decompensation.

decision_points.prompt: Before attributing this anemia to prematurity-related hyporegeneration, the team wants to exclude an immune hemolytic process such as HDFN. Which combination of findings supports a non-hemolytic, hyporegenerative etiology rather than HDFN?
options: Positive DAT with elevated bilirubin and abundant spherocytes, confirming ABO HDFN
Negative DAT, non-elevated bilirubin, no significant erythroblastosis or spherocytosis, and negative maternal/newborn antibody screens
An elevated MCA-PSV on Doppler ultrasound confirming fetal anemia
A positive Kleihauer-Betke test confirming fetomaternal hemorrhage
decision_points.answer: Negative DAT, non-elevated bilirubin, no significant erythroblastosis or spherocytosis, and negative maternal/newborn antibody screens
decision_points.rationale: Newborn HDFN workup classically shows a positive DAT (anti-IgG), elevated unconjugated bilirubin, and characteristic morphology - spherocytes predominate in ABO HDFN while erythroblastosis predominates in Rh HDFN (SRC-017, SRC-025). The absence of all of these, combined with negative maternal and newborn antibody screens, argues against an immune hemolytic process and is consistent with a hyporegenerative, EPO-driven anemia of prematurity instead.

decision_points.prompt: The infant (birth weight 1100 g) remains symptomatic and the team decides to transfuse. What unit specifications, volume strategy, and pretransfusion testing approach are appropriate?
options: Transfuse a standard non-irradiated adult-derived unit using the 1-unit-raises-Hgb-1-g/dL rule, and repeat a full type and screen before this and every subsequent transfusion

Transfuse an irradiated, leukoreduced (CMV-safe-equivalent), small-volume aliquot (10-15 mL/kg) from a dedicated donor unit; because the initial antibody screen was negative and only group O compatible cells are used, repeat ABO/Rh typing and antibody screens are not required for the rest of the admission
Withhold irradiation since TA-GVHD prevention only applies to intrauterine and exchange transfusions
Perform a double-volume exchange transfusion instead of a simple transfusion
decision_points.answer: Transfuse an irradiated, leukoreduced (CMV-safe-equivalent), small-volume aliquot (10-15 mL/kg) from a dedicated donor unit; because the initial antibody screen was negative and only group O compatible cells are used, repeat ABO/Rh typing and antibody screens are not required for the rest of the admission
decision_points.rationale: Irradiation is mandatory for premature neonates <1200 g to prevent TA-GVHD (SRC-017, SRC-025). Universal leukoreduction is considered equivalent to CMV-seronegative status for preventing CMV transmission to neonates (SRC-025). Simple neonatal transfusions use small-volume aliquots (10-15 mL/kg) from dedicated units to minimize donor exposure (SRC-017). Because the neonate's initial antibody screen was negative and only group O compatible cells are being given, repeat ABO/Rh typing and antibody screens may be omitted for the entire admission through 4 months of age (SRC-017, SRC-025).

cases.answer_key: Recognize that the anemic, symptomatic, hyporegenerative (low-reticulocyte) picture in this very preterm neonate reflects the physiologic blunted erythropoietin (EPO) response to anemia seen in neonates under 4 months of age - i.e., anemia of prematurity - rather than an ongoing hemolytic process. Exclude HDFN using a negative DAT, non-elevated bilirubin, unremarkable smear (no spherocytosis/erythroblastosis), and negative maternal/newborn antibody screens. Manage with an irradiated (mandatory because birth weight <1200 g, to prevent TA-GVHD), leukoreduced (CMV-safe-equivalent), small-volume aliquot (10-15 mL/kg) from a dedicated donor unit to limit donor exposure; because the initial antibody screen was negative and only group O compatible cells are used, ABO/Rh typing and antibody screens do not need to be repeated for the remainder of the admission (through 4 months of age).
cases.teaching_explanation: Erythropoietin is the principal regulator of red cell production, driving marrow reticulocytosis in response to tissue hypoxia (SRC-024; EPO regulation is listed as a Major Concept under the RBCs and RBC Components domain in SRC-010). In neonates under 4 months of age, this feedback loop is physiologically blunted - the EPO response to anemia is attenuated compared to older infants and adults - so a preterm neonate can become significantly anemic and symptomatic (tachycardia with pulse >100, tachypnea with respiration >30, poor weight gain, increased oxygen requirement) without mounting an adequate reticulocyte response (SRC-017, SRC-025). This hyporegenerative pattern, termed anemia of prematurity, must be distinguished from an actively hemolytic process such as HDFN, which instead produces a positive DAT, elevated bilirubin, and characteristic morphology (spherocytes in ABO HDFN, erythroblastosis in Rh HDFN). Once a hemolytic etiology is excluded, management of symptomatic anemia of prematurity draws on neonate-specific transfusion practice rather than adult transfusion rules: irradiation is mandatory below 1200 g birth weight to prevent TA-GVHD, leukoreduction substitutes for CMV-seronegative status, small-volume aliquots from a dedicated unit limit donor exposure given the neonate's small blood volume, and - if the initial screen was negative and only group O compatible cells are used - repeat compatibility testing can be safely omitted for the remainder of the admission.
pitfalls: Misreading a hyporegenerative (low) reticulocyte response in a preterm neonate as bone marrow failure or occult hemolysis instead of recognizing the physiologic blunted EPO response characteristic of neonates under 4 months (SRC-017, SRC-025).

Extrapolating adult restrictive-transfusion trigger evidence (TRICC, FOCUS; ~7-8 g/dL for stable patients, SRC-024) directly onto neonatal decision-making without accounting for neonates' distinct EPO physiology, small blood volume, and symptom burden (SRC-017, SRC-025).
Forgetting mandatory irradiation for an infant under 1200 g at birth, risking TA-GVHD from a non-irradiated cellular component (SRC-017, SRC-025).

Reflexively repeating ABO/Rh typing and antibody screens on every neonatal transfusion encounter, when source-grounded practice allows omission through 4 months of age if the initial screen was negative and only group O compatible cells are used (SRC-017, SRC-025).

source_references.supports: Neonates under 4 months have small blood volumes and a blunted EPO response to anemia; anemia of prematurity presents with tachycardia, tachypnea, poor weight gain, or increased oxygen requirement; HDFN workup shows positive DAT, elevated bilirubin, and characteristic morphology (spherocytes in ABO HDFN, erythroblastosis in Rh HDFN); irradiation mandatory for neonates <1200 g; simple transfusions

Confirms neonates <4 months have immature organ systems, small blood volumes, and a blunted EPO response to anemia; HDFN newborn testing (DAT, morphology) findings; irradiation mandatory for neonates <1200 g; universal leukoreduction is equivalent to CMV-seronegative units for neonates; neonatal testing exemption through 4 months of age.

Red cell production is regulated by erythropoietin; anemia symptoms include tachycardia (pulse >100) and tachypnea (respiration >30); evidence-based restrictive transfusion trigger of 7-8 g/dL (TRICC, FOCUS) applies to stable (adult-trial-derived) patients, providing the contrast point for neonate-specific management.

Establishes erythropoietin regulation as a core Major Concept within the RBCs and RBC Components domain (Red Cell Production subdomain) of the board curriculum, framing this case within the correct blueprint node.

review.notes: All clinical facts, lab patterns, decision-point answers, and pitfalls are traceable to SRC-017, SRC-024, SRC-025 (and SRC-010 for blueprint framing), with several near-verbatim matches: blunted EPO response and ~85-100 mL/kg blood volume in neonates <4 months (SRC-017/025), HDFN morphology contrast (spherocytes=ABO, erythroblastosis=Rh; SRC-017/025), irradiation mandatory <1200g (matches birth weight 1100g; SRC-017/025), leukoreduction as CMV-safe equivalent (SRC-025), 10-15 mL/kg dedicated-unit aliquots (SRC-017 verbatim), and the neonatal testing exemption allowing omission of repeat ABO/Rh

cases.title: Therapeutic Phlebotomy: Distinguishing Manual Blood Removal from Device-Based Apheresis and Grading an Intra-Procedure Vasovagal Reaction
cases.clinical_setup: A 54-year-old man with hereditary hemochromatosis presents to the transfusion medicine/apheresis unit for the fourth session of a planned therapeutic phlebotomy series. He is asymptomatic at check-in, has no acute illness or bleeding, and is scheduled for a manual whole-blood collection into a standard closed collection bag; no cell-separator device is used and no blood components are returned to him during the procedure. Partway through today's collection he develops symptoms that require the team to assess and grade an intra-procedure adverse event.

findings.label: Reason for encounter
findings.value: Fourth session of a planned therapeutic phlebotomy series for hereditary hemochromatosis (chronic iron-overload disorder); no active bleeding or acute illness

findings.label: Procedure type
findings.value: Manual whole-blood venipuncture into a closed collection bag; no cell-separator/extracorporeal device used, no components returned to the patient

findings.label: Pre-procedure quality-system check
findings.value: Trained phlebotomist confirmed, approved collection set inspected, approved arm-scrub antisepsis performed, in-date calibrated scale used, session performed per site phlebotomy SOP

findings.label: Pre-procedure vital signs
findings.value: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, alert and asymptomatic

findings.label: Collection bag target volume
findings.value: Standard whole-blood bag target range (~400-450 mL); under-collection would require low-volume labeling

findings.label: Symptom onset (~400 mL collected)
findings.value: Diaphoresis and lightheadedness reported by the patient

findings.label: Vital signs at symptom onset
findings.value: BP 84/52 mmHg, HR 48 bpm, brief loss of consciousness (seconds) while supine

findings.label: Vital signs 5 minutes after repositioning (legs elevated)
findings.value: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 68 bpm, fully alert and asymptomatic

decision_points.prompt: Before the phlebotomist accesses the vein, what quality-system inputs must be verified and documented to keep this manual whole-blood collection process controlled, given that a failure at the input stage cascades through all downstream steps?
options: No verification is needed beyond patient identification
Confirm a trained phlebotomist, an approved collection set, approved arm-scrub antisepsis, a calibrated scale, and adherence to the site's phlebotomy SOP
Only the collection bag lot number needs to be checked
Verification is only required for allogeneic blood donation, not therapeutic phlebotomy
decision_points.answer: Confirm a trained phlebotomist, an approved collection set, approved arm-scrub antisepsis, a calibrated scale, and adherence to the site's phlebotomy SOP
decision_points.rationale: QMS logic frames every process as Inputs -> Action -> Output; for a whole-blood collection process the required inputs are a trained phlebotomist, an approved collection set, an approved arm-scrub solution, a calibrated scale, and current phlebotomy SOPs. Because blood bank processes are highly interrelated, controlling these inputs is the most effective way to prevent a cascading, system-wide failure downstream.

decision_points.prompt: The apheresis medical director is asked whether this therapeutic phlebotomy order needs a formal ASFA category (I-IV) assignment before it can be scheduled, the way a TTP plasma exchange or a sickle cell red cell exchange would. How should this be answered?
options: Yes - all blood-removal procedures performed in an apheresis unit require an ASFA category

No - apheresis is defined as blood passed through a medical device to separate and return components, and therapeutic phlebotomy has no separation device and no component return, so it sits outside the ASFA category framework
Yes, but only Category III applies by default
No category is ever required for any procedure performed in the apheresis unit
decision_points.answer: No - apheresis is defined as blood passed through a medical device to separate and return components, and therapeutic phlebotomy has no separation device and no component return, so it sits outside the ASFA category framework
decision_points.rationale: The approved source defines apheresis as a procedure in which blood is passed through a medical device to separate one or more components and return the remainder to the patient, and frames ASFA Category I-IV rankings around exactly that kind of device-based indication (e.g., TPE for TTP as Category I, red cell exchange in sickle cell disease). Therapeutic phlebotomy removes a unit of whole blood by simple venipuncture with no separation device and no return step, so it is procedurally and administratively distinct from ASFA-ranked apheresis indications.

decision_points.prompt: This is the patient's fourth session in an ongoing phlebotomy series. What should be checked before proceeding today, and why does this matter for a repeated blood-removal procedure rather than a single draw?
options: Nothing further - the series should run on a fixed calendar schedule regardless of interval findings
Reassess hemoglobin/hematocrit and the patient's clinical status (not just a lab number) before continuing, because repeated phlebotomy is a recognized, preventable driver of iatrogenic anemia
Only recheck ABO/Rh type before each session
Only check platelet count before each session
decision_points.answer: Reassess hemoglobin/hematocrit and the patient's clinical status (not just a lab number) before continuing, because repeated phlebotomy is a recognized, preventable driver of iatrogenic anemia
decision_points.rationale: Patient blood management teaches that a significant portion of patient anemia is iatrogenic, caused by frequent phlebotomy, and that blood-removal decisions should integrate clinical signs and symptoms and comorbidities rather than an absolute laboratory value alone ('treating the patient, not the value'). Applied to a therapeutic phlebotomy series, the same principle means hemoglobin/hematocrit and clinical reserve should be reassessed before each session so the series can be paused or spaced out if cumulative blood removal is outpacing the patient's marrow reserve.

decision_points.prompt: Roughly 400 mL into today's collection, the patient becomes diaphoretic and lightheaded; BP falls to 84/52 mmHg, HR falls to 48 bpm, and he has a brief loss of consciousness that resolves within seconds once he is supine with his legs elevated. He denies perioral tingling, and five minutes later he is fully alert with normal vital signs. How should this event be classified, and does citrate toxicity belong on the differential?
options: Grade 3 adverse event requiring hospitalization; citrate toxicity is the likely cause
Grade 1 adverse event (vasovagal reaction) that resolved with minimal/no intervention; citrate toxicity is not expected because this simple phlebotomy has no anticoagulated component-return step
Grade 4 adverse event requiring immediate resuscitation
This event cannot be graded because standardized severity tools only apply to allogeneic blood donors
decision_points.answer: Grade 1 adverse event (vasovagal reaction) that resolved with minimal/no intervention; citrate toxicity is not expected because this simple phlebotomy has no anticoagulated component-return step
decision_points.rationale: Vasovagal reactions - hypotension, bradycardia, and sometimes syncope - are described as the most common adverse event associated with needle-based blood removal, and Grade 1 on the standardized AABB/ISBT severity tool is defined as resolving with minimal or no intervention and no limitation on activities of daily living, matching this patient's rapid, self-limited recovery. Citrate toxicity, by contrast, is specific to apheresis procedures because it results from calcium chelation by the anticoagulant used to process and return components; simple therapeutic phlebotomy collects whole blood into a bag with no extracorporeal anticoagulation-and-return circuit, so citrate-mediated symptoms such as perioral tingling are not expected - consistent with their absence here.

cases.answer_key: Confirm QMS inputs (trained phlebotomist, approved collection set, approved arm-scrub antisepsis, calibrated scale, SOP adherence) before starting the collection -> recognize that this is manual therapeutic phlebotomy, not a device-based, ASFA-categorized apheresis procedure, so no ASFA category assignment is required -> reassess hemoglobin/hematocrit and clinical reserve before proceeding with each session in the series to avoid inducing iatrogenic anemia -> when the patient becomes transiently hypotensive, bradycardic, and briefly syncopal during collection, correctly grade the event as a self-limited Grade 1 vasovagal reaction per the standardized donor/procedural adverse-event severity tool, and exclude citrate toxicity, which is specific to apheresis procedures with anticoagulated component return and does not occur with simple whole-blood phlebotomy.
cases.teaching_explanation: Board questions on therapeutic phlebotomy frequently test whether the candidate can separate it, both conceptually and administratively, from device-based therapeutic apheresis. Apheresis is explicitly defined as passing blood through a device that separates and returns components, and the ASFA category system (I-IV) is built around exactly that kind of procedure; a manual whole-blood collection - even when performed for a therapeutic indication such as iron overload - does not fit that framework and needs no ASFA justification. The same quality-system logic that governs any whole-blood collection process (trained staff, approved supplies, calibrated equipment, SOP adherence as controlled "inputs") applies to therapeutic phlebotomy, and control at that input stage is what prevents cascading downstream failures. Because therapeutic phlebotomy repeatedly removes red cell mass over a series, it carries the same iatrogenic-anemia risk that patient blood management principles warn about for frequent phlebotomy, so hemoglobin/hematocrit and clinical status should be reassessed before continuing rather than running the series purely on a fixed schedule. Finally, when a reaction occurs during collection, the standardized severity-grading vocabulary built around needle-based blood-removal adverse events - vasovagal reactions as the most common event, Grade 1 defined as resolving with minimal/no intervention - provides the framework for classifying it, while recognizing that citrate toxicity, a chelation phenomenon tied specifically to anticoagulated component return in apheresis, does not apply to a procedure that never returns anything to the patient.
pitfalls: Conflating "apheresis" with "phlebotomy" and inappropriately trying to assign an ASFA category (I-IV) to a manual therapeutic phlebotomy order, when ASFA logic governs device-based separation-and-return procedures.

Attributing a vasovagal reaction's symptoms (or any perioral tingling) to citrate toxicity during therapeutic phlebotomy - citrate toxicity requires an anticoagulated, extracorporeal component-return circuit that simple whole-blood phlebotomy does not have.
Proceeding with a scheduled phlebotomy session purely "by protocol" without reassessing hemoglobin/hematocrit and clinical symptoms, risking iatrogenic anemia from cumulative blood removal.

Over-grading a rapidly self-limited vasovagal episode (resolves in seconds with repositioning, no lasting sequelae) as a severe (Grade 3 or higher) event; standardized criteria reserve higher grades for events requiring hospitalization, prolonged disability (>6 months), or life-threatening intervention.

source_references.supports: Apheresis is defined as blood passed through a medical device to separate one or more components and return the remainder to the patient, and ASFA Category I-IV logic is built around that device-based framework (e.g., TPE for TTP as Category I) - the basis for distinguishing it from manual therapeutic phlebotomy.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 57, Common Reaction Logic
source_references.supports: Citrate toxicity is specific to apheresis donors, resulting from calcium chelation by the anticoagulant used during component processing/return, and resolves with oral calcium.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 57, Five Severity Grades / Common Reaction Logic
source_references.supports: Vasovagal reactions (hypotension, bradycardia, sometimes syncope) are the most common adverse event of needle-based blood removal, and the AABB/ISBT Grade 1 tier is defined as resolving with minimal/no intervention and no ADL limitation.

source_references.locator: Subdomain Node 17, Laboratory Findings and Common Board Traps
source_references.supports: A significant portion of patient anemia is iatrogenic, caused by frequent phlebotomy, and blood-removal decisions should integrate clinical signs/symptoms and comorbidities rather than an absolute lab value alone ("treating the patient, not the value").

source_references.locator: Section 2, The Logic of Systems and Processes (Whole Blood Collection Process example)
source_references.supports: QMS process logic (Inputs -> Action -> Output) for a whole-blood collection process requires a trained phlebotomist, approved collection set, approved arm-scrub solution, calibrated scale, and phlebotomy SOPs; controlling inputs prevents cascading downstream failure.

source_references.supports: Standard whole-blood collection bags target a volume such that 300-404 mL in a 450-mL bag must be labeled low-volume, establishing that typical unit collections run in the roughly 400-450 mL range.

cases.title: Bringing a New Apheresis Cell Separator Online: Validation, QC, and Cold-Chain Technology for HPC-A Collection
cases.clinical_setup: A 58-year-old man with multiple myeloma in first remission is scheduled for autologous peripheral blood stem cell (HPC-A) collection by apheresis in preparation for an autologous transplant. The cellular therapy laboratory has just installed a new apheresis cell separator to perform the collection. Across the collection, processing, and preservation steps, the quality manager and technologists must make a series of device- and technology-governance decisions to keep the product safe and usable for eventual infusion.

findings.label: New apheresis cell separator validation status
findings.value: Installation Qualification (IQ) complete per manufacturer specification; Operational Qualification (OQ) and Performance Qualification (PQ) not yet performed; no Medical Director sign-off on the validation summary

findings.label: HPC-A collection outcome
findings.value: Product collected via the new apheresis instrument; volume and gross appearance acceptable

findings.label: Post-collection release QC (TNC count, CD34+ enumeration, viability assay, microbial sterility)
findings.value: Not yet performed; technologist proposes proceeding directly to cryopreservation

findings.label: Proposed processing environment for volume reduction / CD34+ selection
findings.value: Open bench, outside any biological safety cabinet

findings.label: Proposed storage/transport for a planned ~3-week hold before conditioning is complete
findings.value: Dry ice (-78.5°C) for the full duration; no validation on file supporting dry-ice hold at this length

decision_points.prompt: The new apheresis cell separator has been installed and calibrated by the vendor per manufacturer specification. Is it ready to collect this patient's clinical HPC-A product?
options: Yes - installation and calibration (IQ) alone are sufficient for clinical use
No - Operational Qualification (OQ) and Performance Qualification (PQ) must also be completed, with final Medical Director approval of the validation summary, before clinical use
Yes - a manufacturer certificate of compliance substitutes for facility-level validation
No - validation is only required for instruments used in allogeneic collections
decision_points.answer: No - Operational Qualification (OQ) and Performance Qualification (PQ) must also be completed, with final Medical Director approval of the validation summary, before clinical use
decision_points.rationale: Process validation for new equipment/technology requires three phases: IQ (installed per specifications in an appropriate environment), OQ (process operates as intended, including worst-case challenges), and PQ (consistently performs under normal working conditions). The Medical Director must give final approval of the validation summary before the process or equipment is implemented for clinical use.

decision_points.prompt: The HPC-A product has been successfully collected. What must be completed before the product can be released for cryopreservation?
options: ABO/Rh typing and antibody screen only
Total nucleated cell (TNC) count, CD34+ enumeration, viability assay, and microbial sterility testing
HLA typing and physical crossmatch only
A coagulation panel (PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen)
decision_points.answer: Total nucleated cell (TNC) count, CD34+ enumeration, viability assay, and microbial sterility testing
decision_points.rationale: Quality control on HPC-A products mandates assessment of total nucleated cell (TNC) counts, CD34+ enumeration, viability assays, and microbial sterility before the product is considered releasable.

decision_points.prompt: The technologist plans to perform volume reduction and CD34+ selection on the open collection bag on an open bench to save time. Is this acceptable?
options: Acceptable - open bench processing is fine as long as gloves are worn
Not acceptable - only a Class I Biological Safety Cabinet is needed, since it protects personnel
Not acceptable - a Class II Biological Safety Cabinet is required, because it protects personnel, environment, AND the product
Not acceptable - the procedure requires a BSL-4 suite
decision_points.answer: Not acceptable - a Class II Biological Safety Cabinet is required, because it protects personnel, environment, AND the product
decision_points.rationale: Class II BSCs protect personnel, environment, and product and are the standard for blood bank open-processing and cellular therapy; this aligns with BSL-2 containment requirements that govern most blood bank/cellular therapy laboratory activities.

decision_points.prompt: The product must stay viably preserved for about 3 weeks until the patient completes conditioning chemotherapy. The technologist cryopreserves it with a DMSO-based cryoprotectant using controlled-rate cooling, then proposes storing/shipping it on dry ice (-78.5°C) for the entire 3-week interval for convenience. Is this appropriate?
options: Yes - dry ice is an acceptable substitute for liquid nitrogen for any storage duration
No - long-term frozen storage should default to liquid nitrogen vapor phase (-196°C); dry ice is appropriate only if specifically validated for that shorter duration
No - DMSO cryoprotectant should never be used for HPC-A products
Yes - as long as the product is thawed within 24 hours of collection
decision_points.answer: No - long-term frozen storage should default to liquid nitrogen vapor phase (-196°C); dry ice is appropriate only if specifically validated for that shorter duration
decision_points.rationale: Cryopreservation uses cryoprotectants (e.g., DMSO) and controlled-rate cooling to prevent cellular damage. Maintenance of the cold chain is critical: products must be transported in liquid nitrogen vapor phase (-196°C) for frozen storage, or via dry ice (-78.5°C) only if specifically validated for shorter durations, followed by bedside thawing and immediate infusion with rigorous monitoring for adverse reactions.

cases.answer_key: Correct pathway: (1) A newly installed apheresis cell separator cannot be used clinically on installation/calibration alone — full three-phase validation (IQ → OQ → PQ) must be completed, with final Medical Director approval of the validation summary, before it collects a clinical HPC-A product. (2) After collection, the product cannot go straight to cryopreservation — mandatory release QC (TNC count, CD34+ enumeration, viability assay, microbial sterility) must be performed first. (3) Any open manipulation of the product (volume reduction, CD34+ selection) must occur inside a Class II Biological Safety Cabinet, which protects personnel, environment, AND product (not a Class I, which protects only personnel/environment) — consistent with BSL-2 standards for blood bank/cellular therapy processing. (4) Cryopreservation uses a DMSO-based cryoprotectant with controlled-rate cooling; for a multi-week hold, the product must be maintained in liquid nitrogen vapor phase (-196°C) for reliable long-term frozen storage — dry ice (-78.5°C) is acceptable only if specifically validated for that shorter duration — followed by bedside thaw and immediate infusion with rigorous monitoring for adverse reactions.
cases.teaching_explanation: In modern cellular therapy/apheresis programs, "current devices and technology" is inseparable from the quality management system that governs them. Bringing a new apheresis cell separator online is not complete at installation: the facility must walk through the full three-phase validation pathway (IQ confirms correct installation and environment; OQ demonstrates the process works as intended including worst-case challenges; PQ confirms consistent performance under real clinical conditions), and the Medical Director must sign off before the instrument touches a clinical collection. Once a product is collected, release is gated on defined QC (TNC, CD34+, viability, sterility) — QC is not optional or deferrable to "whenever convenient." Any open manipulation of the product (volume reduction, CD34+ selection, T-cell depletion) must happen inside a Class II BSC, the blood bank/cellular-therapy standard, because it uniquely protects the product itself in addition to personnel and environment (unlike Class I). Finally, the preservation technology chain — DMSO cryoprotectant plus controlled-rate cooling, then a validated cold chain (LN2 vapor phase at -196°C for standard frozen storage, dry ice at -78.5°C only when validated for a shorter interval) — determines whether the product remains viable through bedside thaw and infusion. Ongoing equipment QC (e.g., centrifuge speed/timer checked quarterly, centrifuge function checked yearly) keeps an apheresis instrument in a state of control after initial validation. Related but distinct "current technology," pathogen inactivation systems (amotosalen/UVA, riboflavin/UV, UVC) apply this same technology-governance mindset to apheresis-collected platelet and plasma components rather than to cellular therapy products.
pitfalls: Assuming installation and vendor calibration (IQ) alone qualify a new apheresis device for clinical use; OQ, PQ, and Medical Director sign-off on the validation summary are also required.
Confusing BSC classes: selecting a Class I cabinet (protects personnel/environment only) when Class II is required because it also protects the product, which is essential for open HPC-A processing.
Treating dry ice (-78.5°C) as an unrestricted substitute for liquid nitrogen vapor phase (-196°C) storage; dry ice is only appropriate for durations specifically validated for it.
Proceeding to cryopreservation or infusion before mandatory release QC (TNC, CD34+ enumeration, viability, sterility) is completed and documented.

source_references.supports: HPC-A apheresis collection, specialized processing (volume reduction, CD34+ selection, T-cell depletion), DMSO/controlled-rate cryopreservation, mandatory release QC (TNC, CD34+, viability, sterility), LN2 vapor (-196°C) vs validated-duration dry ice (-78.5°C) cold chain, bedside thaw/infusion monitoring, and the IQ/OQ/PQ validation pathway requiring Medical Director approval.

source_references.locator: Domain 1 Technical & Serological Logic study guide, sections 3 and 6
source_references.supports: Three-phase process validation logic (IQ/OQ/PQ) with worst-case challenge testing, and the Class I vs Class II vs Class III biosafety cabinet comparison establishing Class II as the blood bank/cellular therapy open-processing standard because it protects personnel, environment, and product.

source_references.locator: Domain 2 Clinical Practice study guide, sections 2 and 4
source_references.supports: Parallel validation/QC governance (IQ/OQ/PQ, Medical Director approval) plus periodic equipment QC intervals for centrifuges (speed/timer quarterly, function yearly) relevant to ongoing apheresis instrument upkeep after initial validation, and confirmation that BSL-2 is standard for blood bank laboratory activities.

cases.title: Early Neonatal Jaundice in a First Pregnancy: Recognizing ABO HDFN
cases.clinical_setup: A 20-hour-old term male infant develops visible jaundice on the newborn nursery. He was delivered vaginally without complication to a 28-year-old G1P1 mother (this is her first pregnancy) whose prenatal blood type is O, Rh-positive, with a documented negative antibody screen at her first prenatal visit and again at admission for delivery. The infant is well-appearing but visibly icteric on exam, without hepatosplenomegaly, and there were no signs of hydrops fetalis on prior prenatal ultrasounds. The infant's blood type returns as A, Rh-positive.

findings.label: Maternal antibody screen (prenatal and at delivery)
Newborn hemoglobin/hematocrit at birth
findings.value: Within normal limits for gestational age

findings.label: Peripheral blood smear
findings.value: Spherocytes present; no significant erythroblastosis (nucleated RBCs not prominent)

decision_points.prompt: Given the mother is O-positive with a negative antibody screen and this is her first pregnancy, what is the most likely cause of this newborn's early jaundice, and how can it occur in a first pregnancy at all?
options: Rh HDFN from anti-D sensitization by an earlier, undetected fetomaternal hemorrhage
ABO HDFN due to naturally occurring maternal anti-A
Physiologic jaundice of the newborn
Breastfeeding-associated jaundice
decision_points.answer: ABO HDFN due to naturally occurring maternal anti-A
decision_points.rationale: Anti-A and anti-B are naturally occurring antibodies, not induced by prior antigen exposure, so ABO HDFN — unlike Rh HDFN, which requires prior maternal sensitization — can occur in a first pregnancy (SRC-017: 'ABO HDFN Timing: Unlike Rh HDFN, ABO HDFN can occur in the first pregnancy because anti-A and anti-B are naturally occurring'). The mother's negative antibody screen does not exclude this: prenatal antibody screens are performed against reagent O-cell panels (SRC-024: antibody screening uses 'reagent O-cell sets'), which lack A and B antigens and therefore cannot detect anti-A/anti-B.

decision_points.prompt: The newborn's labs show a weakly positive DAT, normal Hgb/Hct at birth, elevated unconjugated bilirubin, and spherocytes on smear without significant erythroblastosis. How does this pattern support ABO HDFN over Rh HDFN, and what does it imply about severity?
options: This pattern is inconsistent with any hemolytic process and suggests sepsis instead
This pattern is classic for Rh HDFN, which is typically severe
This pattern is classic for ABO HDFN and supports a mild clinical course
The DAT result excludes HDFN entirely
decision_points.answer: This pattern is classic for ABO HDFN and supports a mild clinical course
decision_points.rationale: Per SRC-017's comparison point, 'Rh is typically severe (anemia at birth); ABO is typically mild (normal hemoglobin at birth, delayed jaundice)' — this infant's normal birth Hgb fits ABO HDFN, not Rh HDFN. Morphologically, 'spherocytes are common in ABO HDFN but rare in Rh HDFN (where erythroblasts predominate)' (SRC-017), matching this smear. The only weakly positive DAT is also consistent with the milder ABO picture, paralleling the recognized phenomenon that ABO antigen expression/reactivity is comparatively weak in newborns (SRC-024: 'Missing/Weak antigens: Seen in newborns').

decision_points.prompt: With no anemia at birth, no hydrops, and isolated elevated unconjugated bilirubin, what is the appropriate first-line management, and what is the rationale for treating at all?
options: Immediate double-volume exchange transfusion
Phototherapy (460–490 nm) as first-line, with IVIG as an adjunct if bilirubin continues to rise
No treatment needed; discharge and outpatient follow-up only
Simple top-up transfusion of 10–15 mL/kg
decision_points.answer: Phototherapy (460–490 nm) as first-line, with IVIG as an adjunct if bilirubin continues to rise
decision_points.rationale: SRC-017 lists neonatal HDFN therapy as 'Phototherapy (460–490 nm) to conjugate bilirubin; IVIG to block Fc receptors; or double-volume exchange transfusion to remove bilirubin and antibodies' — phototherapy is the appropriate first step for a mild presentation, with IVIG reserved for bilirubin trending toward exchange-level concern. Treatment is warranted because unconjugated bilirubin, if allowed to rise unchecked, risks kernicterus, defined in SRC-017 as 'permanent brain damage caused by toxic levels of unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin in the neonatal brain.'

decision_points.prompt: Suppose bilirubin continued to rise despite intensive phototherapy and IVIG, requiring double-volume exchange transfusion. What special requirements apply to the red cell component, and what is a common pitfall when interpreting this newborn's initial ABO/Rh typing?
options: No special component requirements apply since the infant is otherwise healthy
Components must be irradiated and CMV-reduced-risk; a weak/missing antigen reaction on newborn typing should not automatically be read as a true discrepancy or as 'blocked' antigen sites
Only irradiation is required; CMV status is irrelevant in term infants
The infant should receive uncrossmatched group AB cells for the exchange
decision_points.answer: Components must be irradiated and CMV-reduced-risk; a weak/missing antigen reaction on newborn typing should not automatically be read as a true discrepancy or as 'blocked' antigen sites
decision_points.rationale: SRC-017 states irradiation is 'Mandatory for IUT, exchange transfusion, and premature neonates (<1200g) to prevent TA-GVHD,' and SRC-010 notes neonates (<4 months) 'require specific blood banking practices, such as the use of dedicated units or CMV-reduced components.' Separately, SRC-024 identifies 'Missing/Weak antigens' as an expected ABO discrepancy pattern 'seen in newborns' due to immature antigen expression — this is a distinct, benign phenomenon from the 'blocked Rh' false-negative typing caused by heavy maternal anti-D coating D-antigen sites, which SRC-017 notes is 'resolved by performing an eluate.' Conflating ordinary newborn antigen weakness with true serologic discrepancy or with blocked-antigen phenomena is a recognized board trap.

cases.answer_key: ABO HDFN in an O-mother/A(or B)-infant pair presenting as mild, early (12–48h) jaundice in a FIRST pregnancy — possible because anti-A/anti-B are naturally occurring IgG1/IgG3 antibodies that do not require prior sensitization (unlike Rh HDFN). The lab picture (weakly positive DAT, normal-to-mildly-low Hgb at birth, spherocytes rather than erythroblasts on smear) confirms a mild extravascular hemolytic process typical of ABO HDFN rather than the more severe, anemia-at-birth, erythroblast-predominant picture of Rh HDFN. First-line management is phototherapy (460–490 nm) ± IVIG to block Fc receptors; double-volume exchange transfusion is reserved for bilirubin refractory to these measures, and any cellular component given to the neonate (<4 months) must be irradiated and CMV-reduced-risk.
cases.teaching_explanation: HDFN results from maternal IgG alloantibodies crossing the placenta (only IgG, specifically subclasses IgG1 and IgG3, is actively transported) and destroying fetal/neonatal RBCs that carry the corresponding paternally-inherited antigen, producing extravascular hemolysis, anemia, and compensatory extramedullary hematopoiesis. ABO HDFN is a distinct, generally milder entity within this pathophysiology because the responsible antibodies — anti-A and anti-B — are naturally occurring rather than requiring a sensitizing exposure; this is why, unlike Rh HDFN, ABO HDFN can present in a first pregnancy, and why a negative maternal antibody screen (performed against reagent group O cells that lack A/B antigens) does not rule it out. Clinically and serologically, ABO HDFN tends to diverge from Rh HDFN in several source-grounded ways: onset of jaundice is early (12–48 hours) but generally mild, hemoglobin at birth is typically normal (contrasted with the anemia-at-birth picture of severe Rh HDFN), the DAT is often only weakly positive, and the peripheral smear shows spherocytes rather than the erythroblastosis characteristic of Rh HDFN. Management follows an escalating ladder — phototherapy at 460–490 nm to photoisomerize/conjugate bilirubin, IVIG to blunt Fc-receptor-mediated hemolysis if bilirubin trends upward, and double-volume exchange transfusion reserved for bilirubin unresponsive to these measures — because uncontrolled unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia risks kernicterus. Any cellular component transfused to a neonate under 4 months, including for exchange transfusion, must be irradiated (to prevent TA-GVHD) and CMV-reduced-risk, reflecting the general neonatal transfusion practice standards that apply regardless of the specific cause of HDFN.
pitfalls: Assuming HDFN cannot occur in a first pregnancy — true for Rh HDFN (requires prior sensitization) but false for ABO HDFN, since anti-A and anti-B are naturally occurring antibodies not dependent on prior antigen exposure (SRC-017).

Expecting a strongly positive DAT or anemia at birth in ABO HDFN — the DAT is often only weakly positive and hemoglobin is typically normal at birth in ABO HDFN, in contrast to the more severe, anemia-at-birth phenotype of Rh HDFN (SRC-017).

Misreading a weak or missing ABO antigen reaction on a newborn's forward type as a true serologic discrepancy requiring extensive workup, rather than recognizing it as an expected, benign finding in neonates (SRC-024).

Confusing spherocytosis (the smear finding typical of ABO HDFN) with erythroblastosis/nucleated RBC predominance (the smear finding typical of Rh HDFN) — these point to different diagnoses and different expected severities (SRC-017).

source_references.locator: Board Study Note: Domain III – RBCs and RBC Components
source_references.supports: Antibody screens use reagent group O cells (explaining why maternal screen is negative despite anti-A/B), and newborn RBCs commonly show weak/missing antigen reactivity as a recognized ABO discrepancy pattern.

source_references.locator: Domain VII: Obstetric and Pediatric Patients

source_references.supports: General immunologic principle that isoagglutinins directed against foreign/incompatible RBC antigens ('major' antibody-mediated incompatibility) cause hemolysis of the antigen-bearing cells — used only as background analogy from the HSCT context, not as direct grounding for the neonatal case.

cases.title: Acute Neurologic Complication of Sickle Cell Disease in Pregnancy: RBC Exchange and an Unmasked Partial D
cases.clinical_setup: A 24-year-old G1P0 woman at 30 weeks gestation with known homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) presents to labor and delivery triage with acute-onset right-sided weakness and difficulty speaking, evolving over the preceding hour, in the setting of an ongoing vaso-occlusive pain crisis. Neurology and obstetrics suspect an acute ischemic neurologic event and consult the transfusion service emergently to plan red cell therapy.

findings.label: Neurologic exam
findings.value: Acute-onset right-sided hemiparesis with expressive aphasia, concerning for ischemic stroke

findings.label: Hemoglobin S (HbS) fraction, pre-exchange
findings.value: Elevated, at patient's chronic HbSS baseline (formal quantitation pending)

findings.label: ABO/Rh type (routine serologic testing)
findings.value: Group O, D-positive by tube/gel testing

findings.label: RBC alloantibody screen
findings.value: Pending; patient has a prior transfusion history

findings.label: Molecular RBC genotype (RHD), sent per SCD extended-matching protocol
findings.value: RHD hybrid allele consistent with partial D (DVI-type variant), despite D-positive routine serology

decision_points.prompt: Neurology and obstetrics confirm an acute ischemic process is likely and ask the blood bank what emergent red cell therapy should be initiated, and to what target. What is the most appropriate action?
options: Simple (top-up) RBC transfusion to raise hemoglobin
Therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE)
Emergent erythrocytapheresis (automated RBC exchange) targeting an HbS fraction below 30%
Platelet transfusion
decision_points.answer: Emergent erythrocytapheresis (automated RBC exchange) targeting an HbS fraction below 30%
decision_points.rationale: Red cell exchange is the apheresis procedure specifically used for acute complications of sickle cell disease; it rapidly reduces the HbS fraction below 30% for stroke prevention/treatment, a target and speed of correction a simple top-up transfusion cannot reliably achieve.

decision_points.prompt: The blood bank must select red cell units for this large-volume exchange in a patient with SCD and a prior transfusion history. What selection approach should guide unit choice beyond a routine ABO/Rh crossmatch?
options: Issue random group O units, crossmatch-compatible by immediate spin only
Select extended (phenotypic) antigen-matched units guided by molecular RBC genotyping
Use only directed-donor units from first-degree relatives
Defer any matching strategy since this is an emergency exchange
decision_points.answer: Select extended (phenotypic) antigen-matched units guided by molecular RBC genotyping
decision_points.rationale: Molecular RBC genotyping is essential in sickle cell disease for providing extended antigen matching, since chronically/repeatedly transfused SCD patients are at high risk of alloimmunization; genotyping also identifies clinically important Rh variants that routine serologic typing cannot detect.

decision_points.prompt: The molecular RBC genotype, sent as part of the SCD extended-matching workup, shows that her RHD gene encodes a partial D (DVI-type) hybrid allele, even though routine serology reported her as D-positive. How should this change management of both her red cell transfusion support and her obstetric Rh care?
options: No change; the serologic D-positive result takes precedence for all purposes
She should be considered Rh-negative for transfusion purposes and for RhIg candidacy, to prevent sensitization to the D epitopes missing on her partial D cells
She should be treated as Rh-negative for RhIg only but may continue to receive D-positive units for transfusion
Repeat serologic Rh typing with a different antisera lot and rely on that result alone
decision_points.answer: She should be considered Rh-negative for transfusion purposes and for RhIg candidacy, to prevent sensitization to the D epitopes missing on her partial D cells
decision_points.rationale: Partial D arises from RHCE/RHD hybrid genes producing a qualitative change in the D protein, with some D epitopes absent; patients with partial D can form allo-anti-D if exposed to conventional D-positive cells. Recipients with partial D (e.g., DVI) should therefore be considered Rh-negative for both transfusion and RhIg candidacy, unlike weak D (a quantitative reduction in D-antigen sites), where the individual is unlikely to make anti-D if transfused with D-positive blood.

cases.answer_key: Acute neurologic/vaso-occlusive complication of sickle cell disease (HbSS) in pregnancy → emergent erythrocytapheresis (automated RBC exchange), not simple transfusion, targeting an HbS fraction below 30% → red cell units selected via molecular-genotyping-guided extended antigen matching rather than routine ABO/Rh crossmatch alone, because chronically transfused SCD patients are at high alloimmunization risk → molecular RHD genotyping (sent as part of that extended-matching workup) unmasks a partial D (DVI-type) variant despite a routine D-positive serologic type → patient is managed as Rh-negative for both future red cell transfusion and RhIg candidacy to prevent sensitization to her missing D epitopes, in contrast to weak D (quantitative reduction in D sites), which does not carry this risk.
cases.teaching_explanation: Sickle cell disease produces acute vaso-occlusive and neurologic complications, such as stroke, that the transfusion service manages with red cell exchange rather than simple transfusion, because erythrocytapheresis can rapidly and controllably lower the HbS fraction below the 30% threshold used for stroke prevention/treatment. Because SCD patients typically require ongoing transfusion support and carry an elevated risk of red cell alloimmunization, molecular RBC genotyping is used to provide extended antigen matching beyond routine serology. That same molecular testing also serves a second, safety-critical role in these patients: unmasking RHD variants, such as partial D, that type as conventional D-positive on routine serology but carry epitope differences capable of provoking allo-anti-D. Recognizing partial D and distinguishing it from weak D changes both transfusion selection and RhIg candidacy — partial D patients are managed as Rh-negative, while weak D patients generally are not, because only partial D carries meaningful risk of anti-D sensitization.
pitfalls: Treating a D-positive serologic result as sufficient for Rh management in a chronically transfused SCD patient: some patients with partial D type as conventional D-positive by routine serology yet can still form allo-anti-D and must be managed as Rh-negative for transfusion and RhIg purposes.

Confusing partial D with weak D: weak D is a quantitative reduction in D-antigen sites and these individuals are unlikely to make anti-D if given D-positive blood, whereas partial D is a qualitative loss of D epitopes and carries real anti-D risk — the two are not managed interchangeably.
Reaching for simple top-up transfusion instead of erythrocytapheresis when a rapid, controlled reduction in HbS fraction (target below 30%) is needed for an acute SCD complication such as stroke.

Relying on routine ABO/Rh crossmatch alone for a chronically transfused SCD patient instead of molecular-genotyping-guided extended antigen matching, which both reduces alloimmunization risk and detects clinically significant Rh variants.

source_references.supports: Erythrocytapheresis (red cell exchange) is used in sickle cell disease to rapidly reduce HbS levels below 30% for stroke prevention.

source_references.supports: Molecular RBC genotyping is essential in sickle cell disease for providing extended antigen matching and for identifying Rh variants that appear as conventional D by serology but are at risk for allo-anti-D.

source_references.supports: Partial D (e.g., DVI) is a qualitative epitope loss carrying anti-D risk on exposure to D-positive cells, so partial D recipients should be considered Rh-negative for transfusion and RhIg candidacy; this differs from weak D, a quantitative reduction in D sites where anti-D formation is unlikely.

cases.title: Hyperviscosity from IgM Paraprotein: Immunoglobulin Kinetics and Plasma Exchange Selection
cases.clinical_setup: A 68-year-old man with a known 3-year history of Waldenström macroglobulinemia (lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma producing a monoclonal IgM paraprotein) presents to the emergency department with 2 days of progressively blurred vision, headache, and dizziness. His wife reports he has seemed more confused than usual over the past 24 hours. He denies fever or focal weakness but mentions intermittent epistaxis over the past week. He is hemodynamically stable and afebrile. The hematology/apheresis service is consulted to evaluate for possible emergent therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE).

findings.label: Fundoscopic exam
findings.value: Bilateral retinal hemorrhages with engorged retinal veins

findings.label: Neurologic exam
findings.value: Headache, dizziness, mild confusion; no focal deficits

findings.label: ADAMTS13 activity
findings.value: Normal (not consistent with TTP, which is typically <10%)

decision_points.prompt: Given the retinal hemorrhages, headache, dizziness, and confusion in a patient with known elevated monoclonal IgM, plus a normal platelet count, normal peripheral smear (no schistocytes), and normal ADAMTS13 activity, what is the diagnosis — and why is this NOT TTP despite both conditions being treated with TPE?
options: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)
IgM-driven hyperviscosity syndrome (Waldenström macroglobulinemia)
TRALI from a recent transfusion
Disseminated intravascular coagulation
decision_points.answer: IgM-driven hyperviscosity syndrome from Waldenström macroglobulinemia. It is not TTP because the patient lacks the defining TTP pentad findings (no thrombocytopenia, no microangiopathic hemolysis on smear, and ADAMTS13 activity is normal rather than severely deficient).
decision_points.rationale: SRC-047 defines hyperviscosity syndrome as visual disturbances (retinal hemorrhages), headaches, and dizziness, common in Waldenström macroglobulinemia (IgM); TTP is defined by a pentad (MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neurologic signs, renal dysfunction, fever) with ADAMTS13 activity typically <10%, none of which this patient's normal platelets/smear/ADAMTS13 support.

decision_points.prompt: Why is a single (or very few) session(s) of TPE expected to be highly effective for this patient's IgM-mediated hyperviscosity, whereas apheresis for an IgG-mediated antibody disease typically requires multiple sessions?
decision_points.answer: IgM is a large immunoglobulin that is predominantly intravascular (~80%), so a single TPE session accesses and removes most of the pathogenic IgM directly from the plasma being exchanged. IgG, in contrast, is about 60% extravascular, so tissue-stored IgG re-equilibrates into the vascular space between sessions, requiring repeated exchanges to progressively lower the total body IgG burden.
decision_points.rationale: SRC-047 Common Board Traps and Distractors: 'IgM is primarily intravascular (80%), making TPE highly effective in a single session. IgG is 60% extravascular, necessitating multiple sessions to allow for equilibration.'

decision_points.prompt: What plasma-volume (PV) exchange target should the initial TPE session use, roughly how much of the intravascular IgM will that remove, and should the team plan to escalate directly to a 2.0-PV exchange if symptoms persist after the first session?
decision_points.answer: A standard 1.0-PV exchange, which removes approximately 63%-70% of an intravascular substance like IgM. Escalating immediately to 2.0 PV is not the default next step — doubling the exchanged volume only adds about 20% more removal while doubling procedure time, cost, and risk, so additional 1.0-PV sessions with clinical reassessment are generally preferred over jumping to 2.0 PV.
decision_points.rationale: SRC-047 Core Concepts: 'a 1.0-plasma-volume (PV) exchange removes approximately 63%-70% of an intravascular substance.' Common Board Traps: 'Increasing PV from 1.0 to 2.0 only increases substance removal by an additional ~20% while doubling the time, cost, and risk.'

decision_points.prompt: What replacement fluid should be used for this TPE procedure, and why is FFP not the preferred choice here even though FFP (unlike albumin) actually contains immunoglobulins?
decision_points.answer: 5% human serum albumin is the standard replacement fluid for this indication, selected to minimize allergic/anaphylactoid reactions and disease-transmission risk. FFP is reserved for indications such as TTP, where it is needed to supply ADAMTS13 — despite FFP containing immunoglobulins and coagulation factors that albumin lacks, its higher risk of allergic reaction and citrate load makes it a poor default for routine TPE such as this hyperviscosity case.
decision_points.rationale: SRC-047 Management: 'Most Other TPE: 5% Human Serum Albumin is the standard replacement fluid to minimize allergic reactions and disease transmission risk' versus 'TTP: Emergent TPE with Plasma (FFP) as the replacement fluid to provide ADAMTS13.' High-Yield Comparison: 'Plasma contains all factors but carries high risk of allergic reactions and citrate load; Albumin has low reaction risk but lacks coagulation factors and immunoglobulins.'

cases.answer_key: Diagnose IgM-driven hyperviscosity syndrome from underlying Waldenström macroglobulinemia (not TTP — platelets, smear, and ADAMTS13 are normal, so the TTP pentad is not met). Because IgM is ~80% intravascular, emergent therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE) at a 1.0 plasma-volume (PV) exchange is expected to be highly effective after a single session (removing ~63-70% of intravascular IgM), unlike IgG-mediated antibody disease, which is only 60% extravascular-corrected (i.e., IgG is ~60% extravascular) and needs repeated sessions for equilibration. Escalating to 2.0 PV is not first-line, since it only adds ~20% further removal for double the time/cost/risk. Replacement fluid should be 5% albumin (not FFP) to minimize allergic reaction and citrate/disease-transmission risk; FFP is reserved for ADAMTS13-deficient TTP, even though FFP (unlike albumin) does contain immunoglobulins and coagulation factors.
cases.teaching_explanation: Plasma is a complex fluid whose protein constituents include albumin (oncotic pressure), coagulation factors (hemostasis), and immunoglobulins (immunity) (SRC-031). Immunoglobulin classes are not interchangeable from a kinetics standpoint: IgM is a large, predominantly intravascular molecule (~80% intravascular), so pathologic IgM excess — as in Waldenström macroglobulinemia — produces hyperviscosity syndrome (visual disturbance/retinal hemorrhage, headache, dizziness) and responds dramatically to a single TPE session because most of the pathogenic burden sits in the compartment being exchanged. IgG, by contrast, is about 60% extravascular, so IgG-mediated antibody diseases treated with apheresis require multiple sessions to allow extravascular IgG to re-equilibrate into plasma before it, too, can be removed (SRC-047). TPE volume kinetics follow first-order removal: a 1.0-PV exchange removes ~63%-70% of an intravascular substance, and escalating to 2.0 PV yields only ~20% additional removal for double the time/cost/risk — a "diminishing returns" board trap (SRC-047). Finally, replacement-fluid selection in TPE reflects a genuine trade-off around immunoglobulin (and coagulation factor) content: plasma (FFP) contains immunoglobulins and clotting factors and is required when those factors must be replaced (e.g., ADAMTS13 in TTP), but it carries higher allergic-reaction, citrate-load, and disease-transmission risk; albumin lacks immunoglobulins and coagulation factors but is the default replacement fluid for most other TPE indications, including hyperviscosity syndrome, because it minimizes those risks (SRC-047).
pitfalls: Reflexively choosing FFP as the TPE replacement fluid for any plasma-protein disorder — FFP is reserved for indications like TTP (to replace ADAMTS13), not for paraprotein-removal syndromes such as hyperviscosity, where albumin is standard.

Assuming all immunoglobulin-mediated conditions respond identically to TPE — IgM-driven disease (e.g., Waldenström hyperviscosity) responds rapidly to a single exchange because IgM is largely intravascular (~80%), whereas IgG-mediated disease needs repeated sessions because ~60% of IgG is extravascular and re-equilibrates between treatments.
Escalating the exchanged plasma volume from 1.0 PV to 2.0 PV expecting proportional benefit — this adds only ~20% more removal while doubling time, cost, and risk.

Conflating hyperviscosity syndrome with TTP because both present with neurologic symptoms and are treated with TPE — a normal platelet count, absence of schistocytes/MAHA, and normal ADAMTS13 activity argue against TTP and instead point to a paraprotein-driven hyperviscosity state.
